lessons.title: What Economics Actually Is
lessons.content:
Economics is the study of how people, businesses, and governments make decisions when resources are limited. That limitation is the starting point for everything else in this subject.

Resources are scarce. There is only so much land, time, money, labor, and raw material available at any moment. Because of this scarcity, every choice involves a tradeoff. When you spend an hour studying, you are not spending that hour sleeping or working. When a government spends money on roads, that money is not being spent on schools. Every decision to do one thing is simultaneously a decision not to do something else.

Economics is split into two main branches. Microeconomics focuses on the decisions of individuals, households, and firms. It asks questions like: why does the price of coffee rise when a frost hits Brazil? How do firms decide how much to produce? How do wages get determined?

Macroeconomics zooms out and looks at entire economies. It asks: why does a country's unemployment rate rise during a recession? What causes inflation? How do interest rates affect economic growth?

Both branches use models. A model is a simplified representation of reality that isolates specific relationships and helps us think clearly. No model captures everything, and that is by design. The goal is clarity, not completeness.

A key distinction runs through all of economics: positive analysis versus normative analysis. Positive economics describes what is, using evidence and logic. Normative economics prescribes what ought to be, based on values. Whether a minimum wage causes unemployment is a positive question. Whether a minimum wage is good policy involves normative judgment. Good economists are precise about which kind of claim they are making.

examples.label: Scarcity in Practice
examples.code:
A city has a budget of $10 million for public services.

Option A: Build a new hospital wing.
Option B: Repair 50 miles of road.
Option C: Hire 200 new teachers.

Choosing one means giving up the others.
This is scarcity forcing a tradeoff.
Economics studies how and why that choice gets made.

examples.label: Positive vs Normative
examples.code:
Positive: 'A 10% increase in the minimum wage
is associated with a 1-3% reduction in teen employment
in this dataset.'

Normative: 'The government should raise the minimum wage
because workers deserve a living wage.'

The first is testable with data.
The second involves a value judgment about fairness.
Both matter, but they are different kinds of claims.

lessons.title: Opportunity Cost
lessons.content:
The opportunity cost of a choice is the value of the next best alternative you gave up by making that choice. It is not just what you spent. It is what you could have had instead.

If you have $1000 and you spend it on a vacation, the opportunity cost is not just $1000. It is whatever that $1000 would have produced if you had used it differently. If the next best option was investing it at 8% interest, the opportunity cost is $1080 next year.

Opportunity cost applies to time as well. A doctor who decides to mow their own lawn instead of hiring someone has a very high opportunity cost. The hour they spent mowing is an hour they could have spent seeing patients and earning several hundred dollars.

This is why economists say there is no such thing as a free lunch. Even if something has no monetary price, it consumes time, attention, or space that could have gone to something else. The cost is always there. It just is not always obvious.

Understanding opportunity cost is what separates economic thinking from surface-level thinking. Most people count what they spend. Economists also count what they gave up.

One important implication: comparative advantage is entirely built on opportunity cost. Even if one person or country can do everything better than another, both benefit from specializing in the activity where their opportunity cost is lowest. The absolute ability to produce something matters less than the relative cost of producing it.

examples.label: The College Decision
examples.code:
You decide to go to college for 4 years.

Direct costs: $120,000 in tuition and fees.

Opportunity cost: The salary you could have earned
working full-time for 4 years. If that is $35,000/year,
the opportunity cost is $140,000.

Total economic cost of college:
$120,000 + $140,000 = $260,000

Not $120,000. That is why the economic return
on a degree needs to be high to justify the choice.

examples.label: Comparative Advantage via Opportunity Cost
examples.code:
Alice can write 10 pages of code or 5 reports per hour.
Bob can write 4 pages of code or 3 reports per hour.

Alice is absolutely better at both tasks.

Alice's opportunity cost of 1 report = 2 pages of code.
Bob's opportunity cost of 1 report = 4/3 pages of code.

Bob gives up less code to write a report.
Bob has comparative advantage in reports.
Alice should code. Bob should write reports.
Both gain from this specialization.

lessons.title: The Production Possibilities Frontier
lessons.content:
The Production Possibilities Frontier (PPF) is a model that shows the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce when all resources are fully and efficiently used.

Points on the curve represent efficient production. Points inside the curve represent inefficiency. Points outside the curve are currently impossible with available resources and technology.

The shape of the PPF matters. A straight PPF means constant opportunity cost: giving up one unit of good A always produces the same number of units of good B. A bowed-out PPF means increasing opportunity cost: as you produce more of one good, each additional unit costs you more of the other good. This happens because resources are not equally suited to producing all goods. A farmer's land may grow wheat cheaply but only produces steel at enormous opportunity cost because the best farmland gets used first.

The PPF shifts outward when an economy grows. This happens through more resources, better technology, or improved productivity. A technological advance in one sector shifts only part of the curve -- the end associated with that good pivots outward while the other end stays fixed.

The PPF illustrates several core concepts simultaneously: scarcity (you cannot be everywhere on it at once), opportunity cost (the slope at any point), efficiency (being on the curve vs. inside it), and economic growth (the curve moving outward over time).

One insight the PPF makes vivid: an economy can grow without any new resources if it moves from inside the frontier to the frontier. Eliminating inefficiency is itself a form of growth.

examples.label: Reading the PPF
examples.code:
An economy produces only wheat and steel.

At point A: 100 wheat, 0 steel (all resources on wheat)
At point B: 80 wheat, 20 steel
At point C: 50 wheat, 40 steel (balanced production)
At point D: 0 wheat, 50 steel (all resources on steel)

All points A through D are on the frontier: efficient.
A point producing 40 wheat and 10 steel is inside
the frontier: inefficient, resources are being wasted.
A point of 100 wheat and 50 steel is outside: impossible now.

examples.label: Outward Shift from Technology
examples.code:
New harvesting technology only benefits wheat production.

Before: max wheat = 100, max steel = 50.
After: max wheat = 160, max steel still = 50.

The steel endpoint did not move.
The wheat endpoint shifted outward dramatically.
The curve pivoted: more wheat possible at every
level of steel production, but steel capacity unchanged.

A general productivity gain shifts the entire curve out.

lessons.title: Types of Economic Systems
lessons.content:
Every society must answer three fundamental economic questions. What should be produced? How should it be produced? For whom should it be produced? The way a society answers these questions defines its economic system.

A market economy answers these questions through prices and voluntary exchange. Buyers and sellers interact in markets, and prices emerge from those interactions. Prices carry information and coordinate decisions without any central authority directing them. The United States, Canada, and most of Western Europe operate as market economies.

A command economy answers these questions through central planning. A government authority decides what gets produced, in what quantities, and who receives it. The Soviet Union operated this way. Command economies can mobilize resources quickly for specific goals but struggle with the information problem: no central planner has access to all the local knowledge that markets aggregate through prices. Friedrich Hayek argued that this knowledge is dispersed across millions of individuals and cannot be centrally collected -- a fundamental limit on planning.

A mixed economy combines elements of both. Most real economies today are mixed. Governments regulate certain industries, provide public goods, and redistribute income, while markets handle most day-to-day production and exchange.

A traditional economy answers these questions based on custom and historical practice. Production follows patterns established by prior generations. These exist in isolated communities in declining numbers.

No pure form of any system exists in practice. Even the most market-oriented economies involve extensive government roles in property rights enforcement, contract law, and public goods provision. Even the most planned economies relied on black markets and informal price signals to fill gaps the plan missed.

examples.label: Three Questions Across Systems
examples.code:
Question: What to produce?
Market: Whatever buyers are willing to pay for.
Command: Whatever the planning authority decides.
Traditional: Whatever has always been produced.

Question: How to produce it?
Market: Whatever method is most profitable.
Command: Whatever method the plan specifies.
Traditional: Whatever method ancestors used.

Question: For whom?
Market: Those who can afford it.
Command: Whoever the authority allocates to.
Traditional: Distributed by custom and role.

lessons.title: Thinking at the Margin
lessons.content:
Economists think in terms of marginal changes. A marginal change is a small, incremental adjustment to a plan or decision. The question is never should you do this or not. The question is should you do a little more or a little less of this.

Marginal benefit is the additional benefit gained from one more unit of something. Marginal cost is the additional cost incurred by producing or consuming one more unit.

The core principle: a rational decision-maker continues an activity as long as the marginal benefit exceeds the marginal cost. They stop when marginal cost exceeds marginal benefit. The optimal point is where marginal benefit equals marginal cost.

This applies everywhere. How many hours should you study? Keep studying as long as the benefit of one more hour exceeds the cost. How much should a factory produce? Keep producing as long as the revenue from one more unit exceeds the cost of producing it.

Sunk costs are costs that have already been incurred and cannot be recovered. Economic thinking says sunk costs should be ignored in current decisions. What you spent in the past does not change the marginal costs and benefits you face right now. People who keep watching a bad movie because they paid for it are committing the sunk cost fallacy.

The diamond-water paradox illustrates why marginal thinking matters. Water is essential for life; diamonds are not. Yet diamonds cost far more. The resolution: price reflects marginal value, not total value. Water is so abundant that the marginal unit of water has low value. Diamonds are scarce, so the marginal diamond has high value. Total utility is irrelevant to price. Marginal utility determines it.

examples.label: Marginal Decision: How Much to Produce
examples.code:
Unit 1: Revenue $50, Cost $20. Net benefit: +$30. Produce.
Unit 2: Revenue $45, Cost $25. Net benefit: +$20. Produce.
Unit 3: Revenue $40, Cost $35. Net benefit: +$5. Produce.
Unit 4: Revenue $35, Cost $40. Net benefit: -$5. Stop.

The rational firm stops at unit 3.
Unit 4 costs more than it earns.
This is marginal analysis.

examples.label: The Diamond-Water Paradox
examples.code:
Total utility of water over a lifetime: enormous.
Total utility of all diamonds owned: modest.

Marginal utility of one more liter of water: very low.
(You already have plenty.)
Marginal utility of one more diamond: very high.
(You have few or none.)

Price = marginal utility, not total utility.
This is why water is cheap and diamonds are expensive
despite water being far more essential.

questions.q: What is scarcity in economics?
options: A condition where prices become unaffordably high for most consumers in a market.
The condition where resources are limited relative to unlimited human wants, forcing every choice to involve a tradeoff.
A situation specific to developing countries where basic needs are not reliably met.
A temporary market condition where a particular product is in short supply due to a supply disruption.
questions.explanation: Scarcity is the fundamental condition that makes economics necessary. Because resources are finite and wants are not, every choice involves giving something up. Scarcity applies to rich countries and poor ones alike -- even the wealthiest person cannot have unlimited time.

questions.q: What is the opportunity cost of a decision?
options: The total monetary expenditure required to purchase a good or service in the market.
The value of the best alternative that was foregone as a result of making that choice.
The combined value of every alternative that existed but was not chosen.
The psychological regret a person experiences after discovering a better option was available.
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is not what you spent. It is the value of the single next-best thing you could have done instead. It captures the true cost of any choice by asking what was given up, not just what was paid.

questions.q: What does a point inside the Production Possibilities Frontier represent?
options: The most efficient possible allocation of resources given current technology.
A combination of outputs that cannot be achieved with current resources and technology.
An outcome where resources are not being fully or appropriately used, leaving potential output unrealized.
A scenario where the economy is in the process of growing toward a higher frontier.
questions.explanation: Points inside the PPF represent underperformance. The economy is producing less than it is capable of with its available resources and technology. Points on the frontier are efficient. Points outside are currently unattainable.

questions.q: What is marginal cost?
options: The total expenditure incurred in producing every unit of output combined.
The average cost per unit calculated by dividing total cost by quantity produced.
The additional cost incurred by producing exactly one more unit of output beyond the current level.
The cost of producing the very first unit before any economies of scale have been realized.
questions.explanation: Marginal cost is the cost added by producing exactly one additional unit. It is not the average and not the total. Marginal analysis uses this figure to determine optimal output decisions by comparing it against marginal revenue or benefit.

questions.q: What is the sunk cost fallacy?
options: The tendency to over-invest in a venture that is already highly profitable and generating strong returns.
The error of allowing past costs that cannot be recovered to influence current and future decisions.
The habit of underestimating how expensive future projects will turn out to be relative to initial estimates.
The mistake of spending money on assets with no resale value when liquid alternatives exist.
questions.explanation: Sunk costs are gone regardless of what you decide now. Rational economic decisions are based on current marginal costs and benefits. Continuing a bad project because you already invested in it is the sunk cost fallacy -- the past expenditure cannot be recovered either way.

questions.q: What makes an economy a mixed economy?
options: An economy that produces both tangible goods and intangible services across multiple industries.
An economy that combines market-based resource allocation with government intervention, regulation, and public provision.
An economy that conducts trade with countries at different levels of economic development.
An economy that contains both very large multinational corporations and small local businesses simultaneously.
questions.explanation: A mixed economy uses markets for most production and exchange decisions while also involving government through regulation, public goods, and redistribution. Nearly all modern economies are mixed, differing only in the degree of each element.

questions.q: What does it mean to say that labor demand is 'derived demand'?
options: Demand for labor that arises because governments require certain industries to maintain minimum staffing levels.
Demand for labor that comes from the demand consumers have for the goods and services that labor produces.
Demand for labor that is mathematically calculated from productivity statistics and wage data.
Demand for labor that is higher in skilled occupations than in unskilled ones due to specialization.
questions.explanation: Firms do not demand labor for its own sake. They hire workers to produce goods that consumers want to buy. When demand for a product rises, demand for the labor used to make it rises with it. The labor demand is derived from the product market.

questions.q: The diamond-water paradox is resolved by which economic concept?
options: Total utility, since the overall value of water to humanity vastly exceeds that of diamonds across all uses.
Marginal utility, since price reflects the value of one additional unit and water's abundance makes that marginal unit nearly worthless.
Comparative advantage, since diamond producers specialize in the activity where their opportunity cost is lowest.
Derived demand, since the demand for both goods comes from consumer preferences in downstream markets.
questions.explanation: Price reflects marginal utility, not total utility. Water is so abundant that the marginal liter has very low value to you. Diamonds are scarce, so an additional diamond has high value. The paradox dissolves once you distinguish total from marginal value.

lessons.title: Protocol: Calculate Opportunity Cost
lessons.prompt:
You have 3 hours free on Saturday. You can use them to:

Option A: Work a part-time job at $20/hour = $60
Option B: Study for an exam
Option C: Watch movies

You choose to study. What is the opportunity cost of studying?

Write only the dollar amount of the opportunity cost.

validation.message: error: the opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative given up. you chose to study instead of working, so the opportunity cost is the $60 you could have earned. watching movies is not the opportunity cost because it was not the next best option.

lessons.title: Demand
lessons.content:
Demand is the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity that buyers are willing and able to purchase, holding all other factors constant. The key phrase is willing and able. Wanting something is not demand. Having the willingness and the purchasing power together is demand.

The Law of Demand states that as price increases, quantity demanded decreases, and vice versa. This is an inverse relationship. Two mechanisms drive it: the substitution effect (when a good's price rises, consumers switch to cheaper alternatives) and the income effect (a higher price makes consumers effectively poorer, reducing their ability to buy).

A demand curve graphs this relationship with price on the vertical axis and quantity on the horizontal axis. It typically slopes downward from left to right.

It is critical to distinguish between a change in quantity demanded and a change in demand. A change in quantity demanded is a movement along the existing demand curve caused by a price change. A change in demand is a shift of the entire curve caused by something other than price.

Things that shift the demand curve include: income changes, prices of related goods (substitutes or complements), consumer tastes and preferences, expectations about future prices, and the number of buyers in the market.

Substitutes are goods that can replace each other. If the price of butter rises, demand for margarine increases. Complements are goods used together. If the price of printers rises, demand for ink cartridges falls.

Price elasticity of demand measures how responsive quantity demanded is to a price change. Elastic demand means consumers are very responsive -- a small price increase causes a large drop in quantity demanded. Inelastic demand means consumers are not very responsive. Necessities with few substitutes (insulin, gasoline) tend to have inelastic demand. Luxuries with many substitutes tend to have elastic demand.

examples.label: Shift vs Movement
examples.code:
Original demand: At $3/coffee, 200 cups sold per day.

Case 1: Price falls to $2.
Quantity demanded rises to 280 cups.
This is a MOVEMENT along the demand curve.
(Same curve, different point.)

Case 2: Price stays at $3, but incomes rise.
Quantity demanded rises to 260 cups at $3.
This is a SHIFT of the demand curve.
(A new curve entirely -- more demanded at every price.)

examples.label: Elastic vs Inelastic Demand
examples.code:
Insulin (inelastic demand):
Price rises 20%. Quantity demanded falls 2%.
Patients need insulin regardless of price.

Airline tickets to Vegas (elastic demand):
Price rises 20%. Quantity demanded falls 35%.
Consumers delay the trip, drive instead, or skip it.

Elasticity = % change in quantity / % change in price.
Insulin elasticity: 2/20 = 0.1 (very inelastic)
Airline elasticity: 35/20 = 1.75 (elastic)

lessons.title: Supply
lessons.content:
Supply is the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity that producers are willing and able to offer for sale, holding all other factors constant.

The Law of Supply states that as price increases, quantity supplied increases. Higher prices make production more profitable, attracting existing producers to produce more and new producers to enter the market.

As with demand, distinguish between a change in quantity supplied (a movement along the supply curve due to a price change) and a change in supply (a shift of the entire curve).

Factors that shift the supply curve include: input costs (higher wages or material costs reduce supply), technology improvements (increase supply), government taxes and subsidies, prices of related goods in production, number of sellers, and expectations about future prices.

Price elasticity of supply measures how responsive quantity supplied is to price changes. Supply is more elastic when producers can easily scale production up or down. It is more inelastic when production requires specialized capital, long lead times, or fixed natural resources.

In the very short run, supply of many goods is perfectly inelastic: production cannot respond to price changes instantly. Over time, supply becomes more elastic as firms invest in capacity. This is why price spikes following a natural disaster are often temporary: they signal producers to increase supply, which eventually brings prices back down.

examples.label: Supply Shifters
examples.code:
Original supply: At $5/unit, 500 units supplied per day.

Case 1: Input costs rise (oil price spike).
Supply decreases. At $5, only 380 units now offered.
Curve shifts LEFT (less supplied at every price).

Case 2: New automation technology adopted.
Supply increases. At $5, 680 units now offered.
Curve shifts RIGHT (more supplied at every price).

Case 3: Price rises from $5 to $7.
Quantity supplied rises from 500 to 650.
This is a movement along the curve, not a shift.

lessons.title: Market Equilibrium and Price Controls
lessons.content:
Market equilibrium is the price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied. At this price, the market clears: there is no unsold surplus and no unsatisfied demand. The equilibrium price is sometimes called the market-clearing price.

When price is above equilibrium, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded: a surplus exists. Sellers with unsold inventory reduce prices. Price falls toward equilibrium.

When price is below equilibrium, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied: a shortage exists. Buyers compete for limited goods, bidding prices up toward equilibrium.

This self-correcting mechanism is why markets tend toward equilibrium without central direction. It is the price signal at work.

Governments sometimes intervene with price controls. A price ceiling is a legal maximum price set below equilibrium (like rent control). It creates a persistent shortage: quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price. A price floor is a legal minimum price set above equilibrium (like the minimum wage). It creates a potential surplus: quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at the floor price.

Price controls always have secondary effects. Rent control leads landlords to reduce maintenance, convert units to condos, or leave the market. These unintended consequences are a recurring theme in economics: markets route around interference.

examples.label: Surplus and Shortage Mechanics
examples.code:
Equilibrium: Price = $10, Quantity = 500 units.

Price set at $14 (above equilibrium):
Quantity supplied = 700. Quantity demanded = 350.
Surplus = 350 units. Sellers reduce price.
Price falls back toward $10.

Price set at $7 (below equilibrium, e.g. price ceiling):
Quantity supplied = 300. Quantity demanded = 650.
Shortage = 350 units. Buyers compete.
But price is legally capped: shortage persists.
This is why price ceilings cause lasting shortages.

questions.q: What causes a shift in the demand curve rather than a movement along it?
options: A change in the price of the good itself, which causes buyers to purchase more or less of it.
A change in a factor other than price, such as income, preferences, or the price of a related good.
A change in the cost of production inputs that makes the good more or less expensive to manufacture.
A change in the number of firms producing the good, which alters how much is available at each price.
questions.explanation: A movement along the demand curve is caused only by a price change. A shift of the entire demand curve is caused by anything else: income, preferences, prices of substitutes or complements, expectations, or number of buyers.

questions.q: Why does a price ceiling set below equilibrium cause a persistent shortage?
options: Because firms produce more output when prices are controlled, flooding the market beyond what consumers want.
Because the legal maximum price keeps price from rising to the level that would equate quantity supplied with quantity demanded.
Because consumers always buy less when prices are legally controlled, reducing total market activity.
Because suppliers exit the market entirely when any form of government intervention is introduced.
questions.explanation: At a price below equilibrium, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied. Normally, prices would rise to correct this. A ceiling prevents that correction. The shortage is locked in as long as the ceiling remains below the market-clearing price.

questions.q: If consumer incomes fall and the good is a normal good, what happens to the demand curve?
options: It shifts right, because lower incomes make consumers more price-sensitive and eager to buy at any given price.
It shifts left, because lower incomes reduce the quantity consumers are willing and able to purchase at every price.
It remains unchanged, because demand depends on prices rather than on consumer income levels.
It becomes steeper, because lower incomes cause consumers to respond more dramatically to price changes.
questions.explanation: For a normal good, demand rises with income and falls when income falls. The entire curve shifts left: less is demanded at every price level. This is a change in demand, not a change in quantity demanded.

questions.q: When demand is inelastic, what happens to total revenue when price rises?
options: Total revenue falls sharply, because the quantity reduction more than offsets the higher price per unit.
Total revenue rises, because the percentage drop in quantity is smaller than the percentage rise in price.
Total revenue remains constant, because inelastic demand means quantity does not respond to price at all.
Total revenue falls modestly, because consumers substitute toward cheaper alternatives over time.
questions.explanation: With inelastic demand, a price increase causes a proportionally smaller quantity decrease. The revenue gained from the higher price per unit exceeds the revenue lost from selling fewer units. Total revenue rises.

lessons.title: Protocol: Predict the Equilibrium Change
lessons.prompt:
The market for electric vehicles is in equilibrium. Then two things happen simultaneously:

1. The government introduces a $7,500 tax credit for EV buyers.
2. A shortage of lithium raises battery production costs significantly.

What happens to equilibrium price and quantity?

Write your answer in this format: price [rises/falls/ambiguous], quantity [rises/falls/ambiguous].

validation.message: error: the tax credit shifts demand right (increases demand). rising battery costs shift supply left (decreases supply). both shifts raise price, so price rises. but the demand increase raises quantity while the supply decrease reduces quantity -- the net effect on quantity depends on which shift is larger, making it ambiguous without more data.

lessons.title: Utility and Consumer Choice
lessons.content:
Utility is the satisfaction or benefit a consumer derives from consuming a good or service. Economists use utility as a way to model consumer preferences and predict behavior.

Total utility is the overall satisfaction from consuming a given quantity. Marginal utility is the additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit. The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility states that as a person consumes more of a good, the additional satisfaction from each successive unit declines. The first slice of pizza is very satisfying. The fifth slice is far less so.

Consumers maximize utility subject to their budget constraint. They allocate spending across goods until the marginal utility per dollar spent is equal across all goods. If the marginal utility per dollar on apples exceeds that on oranges, the consumer should buy more apples and fewer oranges until they equalize.

This rule -- equalize marginal utility per dollar across all goods -- is the consumer optimum. It explains why people buy different combinations of goods as prices change. When the price of a good rises, its marginal utility per dollar falls, so the rational consumer shifts spending toward other goods. This is why demand curves slope downward.

Consumer surplus is the difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay. If you are willing to pay $50 for a concert ticket and the price is $30, you receive $20 of consumer surplus. Markets generate consumer surplus when prices are below the maximum willingness to pay. Producer surplus is the mirror: the difference between the price a seller receives and the minimum they would accept.

examples.label: Diminishing Marginal Utility
examples.code:
Slices of pizza consumed and marginal utility:

Slice 1: MU = 40 (very hungry, enormously satisfying)
Slice 2: MU = 30 (still good)
Slice 3: MU = 18 (decent)
Slice 4: MU = 8 (you are getting full)
Slice 5: MU = -5 (you feel sick)

Total utility peaks at slice 4 (40+30+18+8=96).
Slice 5 reduces total utility because MU is negative.
Rational consumer stops at slice 4.

examples.label: Consumer Optimum
examples.code:
Budget: $10. Apples: $2 each. Bananas: $1 each.

Apple MU per dollar: 10 utils / $2 = 5 utils/$
Banana MU per dollar: 4 utils / $1 = 4 utils/$

Apples give more utility per dollar.
Buy apples first. After buying apples,
if apple MU falls below banana MU per dollar,
switch to bananas.

Optimum: equalize MU/price across all goods.

questions.q: What does the Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility state?
options: That total utility falls as a consumer buys more goods, because each purchase reduces the budget available for other things.
That the additional satisfaction from each successive unit of a good consumed tends to decline as the quantity consumed increases.
That consumers always prefer lower prices because higher prices reduce the utility they derive from any given purchase.
That markets produce less utility for consumers than central planners could achieve through optimal allocation.
questions.explanation: Diminishing marginal utility says each additional unit adds less satisfaction than the previous one. Total utility can still be rising -- just rising more and more slowly -- until the marginal unit adds nothing or becomes negative.

questions.q: A consumer maximizes utility when:
options: They spend their entire income on the single good that gives the highest total utility over all units consumed.
The marginal utility per dollar spent is equal across all goods they are purchasing.
The marginal utility of every good in their consumption basket is exactly equal to zero.
They minimize the total amount spent while consuming the largest possible quantity of every good.
questions.explanation: The consumer optimum occurs when the last dollar spent on every good yields the same additional utility. If any good gives more utility per dollar than another, the consumer should reallocate spending toward it until the rates equalize.

questions.q: What is consumer surplus?
options: The additional income a consumer earns by working more hours than required for their basic needs.
The difference between what a consumer is willing to pay for a good and what they actually pay for it.
The total amount of money a consumer has left over after purchasing all necessary goods and services.
The benefit consumers receive from government subsidies that make certain essential goods more affordable.
questions.explanation: Consumer surplus measures the gain from trade from the buyer's side. If you would pay $100 for a coat and it costs $60, you receive $40 of consumer surplus. Price controls, monopolies, and taxes all affect how much surplus consumers keep.

lessons.title: Protocol: Utility Maximization
lessons.prompt:
You have $10 to spend. Apples cost $2 each and give 10 units of marginal utility per apple. Bananas cost $1 each and give 4 units of marginal utility per banana.

To maximize utility, should you spend your entire budget on apples, entire budget on bananas, or a mix?

Hint: calculate marginal utility per dollar for each.

Write: apples, bananas, or mix.

validation.message: error: apples give 10 utility per $2 = 5 utility per dollar. bananas give 4 utility per $1 = 4 utility per dollar. apples give more utility per dollar spent, so you should spend everything on apples to maximize utility. the consumer optimum rule says allocate all spending to the good with the highest marginal utility per dollar when that ratio never equalizes.

lessons.title: How Firms Produce
lessons.content:
A firm combines inputs to produce outputs. The main categories of inputs are labor (workers), capital (machinery and equipment), land, and raw materials. The production function describes the relationship between inputs and the quantity of output produced.

In the short run, at least one input is fixed and cannot be changed. A factory cannot be built or demolished instantly. The short run is defined not by a specific number of months but by the presence of at least one fixed input.

In the long run, all inputs are variable. Firms can expand their facilities, enter or exit industries, and adjust every factor of production.

The Law of Diminishing Returns applies in the short run. It states that as you add more of a variable input (like labor) to a fixed input (like a factory), the marginal product of that variable input eventually declines.

Marginal product is the additional output generated by one more unit of input. When you hire the first worker in an empty factory, output rises substantially. As you add more workers, they help each other and specialize, so marginal product rises at first. But eventually the factory gets crowded, workers get in each other's way, and each additional worker adds less than the previous one. That is diminishing returns.

Diminishing returns does not mean output falls. It means the rate of increase slows. This matters because diminishing marginal product directly causes marginal cost to rise. As each additional worker contributes less output, the cost per unit of output produced by that worker is higher. The shape of cost curves is a direct consequence of the production function.

examples.label: Diminishing Returns to Labor
examples.code:
Workers in a factory with 10 machines:

1 worker: Output = 10 units. Marginal product = 10.
2 workers: Output = 22 units. Marginal product = 12.
3 workers: Output = 36 units. Marginal product = 14.
4 workers: Output = 48 units. Marginal product = 12.
5 workers: Output = 58 units. Marginal product = 10.
6 workers: Output = 64 units. Marginal product = 6.
7 workers: Output = 67 units. Marginal product = 3.

Marginal product peaks at 3 workers, then diminishes.
Total output keeps rising but slower and slower.

lessons.title: Types of Costs
lessons.content:
Understanding costs is central to understanding how firms make decisions. Costs have multiple categories that mean different things in different situations.

Fixed costs (FC) do not change with the level of output. Rent on a factory, loan payments on equipment, salaries of permanent staff. Whether you produce 0 units or 1000 units, fixed costs stay the same in the short run.

Variable costs (VC) change with the level of output. Raw materials, hourly labor, electricity used in production. When output doubles, variable costs roughly double as well.

Total cost (TC) is the sum of fixed and variable costs: TC = FC + VC.

Average fixed cost (AFC) is fixed cost divided by quantity. As output rises, fixed costs are spread over more units, so AFC falls continuously. This is the spreading effect.

Average variable cost (AVC) is variable cost divided by quantity. Due to diminishing returns, AVC eventually rises as output increases.

Average total cost (ATC) is total cost divided by quantity. It has a U-shape: AFC pulls it down at first, but eventually rising AVC dominates.

Marginal cost (MC) is the additional cost of producing one more unit. Due to diminishing returns, MC eventually rises as output increases. The MC curve crosses the ATC curve at the ATC minimum point. This is a mathematical necessity: when the cost of the next unit is below the average, it pulls the average down; when it is above the average, it pulls the average up. The crossing must occur at the minimum.

Economic cost includes both explicit costs (actual payments made) and implicit costs (opportunity costs of resources the firm already owns). A firm that owns its own building still incurs the implicit cost of not renting it out. Economic profit subtracts both. Accounting profit subtracts only explicit costs.

examples.label: Cost Structure Example
examples.code:
A bakery has fixed costs of $1,000/month.

Quantity | VC   | TC    | ATC    | MC
0       | $0   | $1000 |   --   | --
100     | $400 | $1400 | $14.00 | $4
200     | $900 | $1900 | $9.50  | $5
300     | $1500| $2500 | $8.33  | $6
400     | $2400| $3400 | $8.50  | $9
500     | $3800| $4800 | $9.60  | $14

ATC is minimized between 300 and 400 units.
MC is rising and crosses ATC at its minimum.

lessons.title: Economies of Scale
lessons.content:
Economies of scale occur in the long run when increasing the scale of production lowers the average total cost per unit. If a firm doubles all inputs and output more than doubles, it has economies of scale.

Sources of economies of scale include: specialization and division of labor at larger scale, the ability to spread fixed costs of machinery over more units, bulk purchasing discounts on inputs, and more efficient use of capital at large volumes.

Diseconomies of scale occur when increasing scale raises average costs. This happens due to management complexity, coordination problems, and bureaucracy in very large organizations.

Constant returns to scale occur when doubling inputs doubles output exactly, leaving average costs unchanged.

The minimum efficient scale is the output level at which a firm first reaches the lowest point on its long-run average cost curve. Industries where minimum efficient scale is very large relative to market demand tend toward having few, very large firms. This is a natural explanation for industry concentration that does not require any anti-competitive behavior.

This concept also explains natural monopoly. When the minimum efficient scale is so large that it encompasses the entire market demand, having more than one firm means each operates on the downward-sloping portion of its cost curve, making average costs higher than if one firm served everyone. The market naturally supports only one efficient producer.

examples.label: Economies vs Diseconomies
examples.code:
A small bakery makes 100 loaves/day. ATC = $3/loaf.
It expands to 1,000 loaves/day. ATC = $1.80/loaf.
Economies of scale: bigger is cheaper per unit.

A massive corporation grows to 100,000 employees.
Coordination costs explode. Every decision takes months.
ATC rises. Diseconomies of scale have set in.

The efficient firm size is where ATC is minimized
before diseconomies outweigh economies.

questions.q: What is the Law of Diminishing Returns?
options: As a firm increases its total output, total costs rise at an accelerating rate regardless of which inputs are used.
As more of a variable input is added to a fixed input, the marginal product of the variable input eventually declines.
As a firm expands its long-run scale, average costs always fall because of specialization and efficiency gains.
As market price rises, quantity supplied falls because higher prices reduce consumer willingness to produce more.
questions.explanation: Diminishing returns is a short-run phenomenon tied to the presence of a fixed input. With at least one fixed input (like a factory), adding more of a variable input (like labor) eventually yields smaller additions to output as the fixed resource becomes a bottleneck.

questions.q: What happens to average fixed cost as output increases?
options: It rises because fixed costs grow proportionally as the firm scales up production to meet higher demand.
It stays constant at the same level because fixed costs by definition do not change with output.
It falls continuously because the same fixed costs are divided across an ever-larger quantity of output.
It first falls then rises in a U-shape as the spreading effect is eventually overtaken by rising variable expenses.
questions.explanation: Fixed costs do not change with output. As output increases, the same fixed cost is divided by a larger and larger number. Average fixed cost falls continuously and never rises. It is average variable cost and average total cost that eventually turn upward.

questions.q: At which output level does the marginal cost curve cross the average total cost curve?
options: At the output level where average total cost is rising at its fastest rate.
At the output level where average total cost reaches its minimum value.
At the output level where marginal cost equals zero and no additional cost is incurred.
At the output level where average total cost equals average fixed cost.
questions.explanation: When MC is below ATC, each new unit brings the average down. When MC rises above ATC, each new unit brings the average up. MC must therefore cross ATC at the exact minimum of the ATC curve. This is a mathematical necessity, not a coincidence.

questions.q: What is the difference between the short run and long run in production theory?
options: The short run covers less than one calendar year while the long run covers any period exceeding twelve months.
In the short run at least one input is fixed; in the long run all inputs can be varied by the firm.
The short run describes the operations of smaller firms while the long run applies to larger multinational corporations.
The short run involves lower total costs than the long run because fewer inputs are being paid for.
questions.explanation: Short and long run are defined by input flexibility, not clock time. In the short run, some inputs (typically capital) are fixed and cannot be adjusted. In the long run, firms can adjust all inputs, including their entire scale of operations.

questions.q: A firm experiences economies of scale. As it increases output, what happens to its long-run average cost?
options: Average costs rise because expanding production increases the burden of both fixed and variable inputs proportionally.
Fixed costs increase as the firm must acquire more capital equipment to support the higher level of output.
Average costs fall as output increases, because expanding production spreads costs and enables greater specialization.
Marginal costs become constant and equal to average costs, eliminating any variation in per-unit expenses.
questions.explanation: Economies of scale mean expanding production lowers average cost per unit. Larger scale enables specialization, bulk purchasing, and more efficient use of capital. The long-run average cost curve slopes downward in the range of economies of scale.

questions.q: What is the difference between economic profit and accounting profit?
options: Economic profit is calculated annually while accounting profit is calculated quarterly for financial reporting purposes.
Economic profit subtracts both explicit costs and implicit opportunity costs; accounting profit subtracts only explicit costs.
Accounting profit includes future projected earnings while economic profit counts only current realized revenue.
Economic profit is reported to regulators and accounting profit is reported to shareholders, using different cost definitions.
questions.explanation: Accounting profit ignores implicit costs -- what the firm's owners could have earned by deploying their resources elsewhere. Economic profit subtracts both. A firm can show positive accounting profit but zero economic profit when implicit opportunity costs equal the accounting surplus.

lessons.title: Protocol: Identify the Cost Type
lessons.prompt:
A restaurant has the following monthly expenses:
- Building lease: $3,000
- Chef salary (fixed): $4,000
- Food ingredients: $8,000 (varies with meals served)
- Electricity for cooking: $1,200 (varies with meals served)

What are the total fixed costs per month? Write only the dollar amount.

validation.message: error: fixed costs are the building lease ($3,000) and the chef salary ($4,000). ingredients and electricity vary with output, so they are variable costs. total fixed costs = $7,000.

lessons.title: Perfect Competition
lessons.content:
Perfect competition is a market structure characterized by many buyers and sellers, identical (homogeneous) products, perfect information, free entry and exit from the market, and no single buyer or seller large enough to influence the price.

In perfect competition, individual firms are price takers. They cannot charge above the market price because consumers can instantly switch to identical products from other sellers. They have no incentive to charge below market price because they can sell everything they produce at the market price.

A perfectly competitive firm maximizes profit by producing the quantity where price equals marginal cost (P = MC). Since price equals marginal revenue in perfect competition, this is the same as the profit-maximizing rule of MR = MC.

In the short run, a firm may make economic profit, break even, or make a loss. In the long run, profits attract entry by new firms, which increases supply and pushes price down until economic profit reaches zero. Losses cause firms to exit, reducing supply and pushing price back up to break even.

The long-run equilibrium in perfect competition is where P = MC = minimum ATC. This is the most efficient market structure: price equals marginal cost (allocative efficiency) and firms operate at minimum average cost (productive efficiency).

Allocative efficiency means resources are allocated to their highest-valued uses. Productive efficiency means goods are produced at the lowest possible cost. Perfect competition achieves both simultaneously in long-run equilibrium, which is why it serves as the benchmark against which other market structures are evaluated.

examples.label: Long-Run Adjustment in Perfect Competition
examples.code:
Initially: Price = $10, ATC = $7. Firms earn profit of $3/unit.
New firms enter the industry, attracted by profit.
Supply increases. Market price falls.
Process continues until: Price = ATC = $7.
Economic profit = $0. No further incentive to enter.

If losses occur: Price = $5, ATC = $7. Loss = -$2/unit.
Firms exit the industry. Supply decreases.
Market price rises.
Process continues until: Price = ATC = $7.
Losses eliminated. No further incentive to exit.

lessons.title: Monopoly
lessons.content:
A monopoly exists when one firm is the sole seller of a product with no close substitutes. The monopolist is not a price taker. It is a price maker. It faces the entire market demand curve and can set the price, but it must accept the lower quantity demanded that comes with a higher price.

Sources of monopoly power include: exclusive ownership of a key resource, patents and copyrights, government licensing, and natural monopoly where one firm can supply the entire market at lower cost than multiple firms could.

A natural monopoly arises when a single firm has such large economies of scale that its average costs keep falling over the entire range of market demand. It makes economic sense to have one firm rather than many. Utilities like water and electricity are classic examples.

The monopolist maximizes profit by producing where MR = MC, then charging the highest price consumers will pay for that quantity, found by looking at the demand curve. Crucially, a monopolist's marginal revenue is always less than price, because to sell one more unit it must lower the price on all units, not just the additional one.

Monopoly produces less output and charges a higher price than perfect competition. This creates deadweight loss: transactions that would have occurred in a competitive market do not occur under monopoly. Deadweight loss represents a net loss to society.

Price discrimination occurs when a monopolist charges different prices to different consumers based on their willingness to pay. A perfect price discriminating monopolist captures all consumer surplus, eliminates deadweight loss, and maximizes its own profit. Real-world examples include airline pricing, student discounts, and pharmaceutical pricing across markets.

examples.label: Monopoly vs Competition
examples.code:
Perfectly competitive outcome:
Price = $10, Quantity = 1,000 units
Price equals marginal cost. No deadweight loss.

Monopoly outcome (same cost structure):
Price = $16, Quantity = 600 units
Monopolist restricts output to raise price.

Losers: consumers who pay more and those
who are priced out of the market entirely.
Deadweight loss: 400 units of beneficial
transactions that no longer occur.

lessons.title: Oligopoly and Monopolistic Competition
lessons.content:
Most real markets fall between the extremes of perfect competition and monopoly.

An oligopoly is a market with a small number of large firms whose decisions are interdependent. Each firm must consider how rivals will respond to its pricing and output decisions. This strategic interdependence is what makes oligopoly analysis require game theory.

The prisoner's dilemma is a classic game theory model that explains why oligopolists often fail to cooperate even when cooperation would benefit all of them. If two firms could collude to both charge high prices, they would both earn high profits. But each firm has an individual incentive to undercut the other. If both follow that incentive, both end up charging lower prices and earning lower profits than if they had cooperated. This is why explicit price-fixing agreements (cartels) form, and why they are illegal.

Game theory also explains the kinked demand curve. An oligopolist believes rivals will match price decreases (preventing it from gaining market share) but not match price increases (letting it lose customers). This makes demand highly elastic above the current price and inelastic below it, creating stability at the prevailing price.

Monopolistic competition involves many firms selling differentiated products. Each firm has some price-setting power because its product is slightly different from rivals, but the market is competitive enough that economic profits are competed away in the long run. Restaurants, clothing brands, and hair salons operate in monopolistically competitive markets.

In monopolistic competition, firms engage in non-price competition: advertising, branding, product design, and service quality to differentiate themselves and maintain some pricing power.

examples.label: Prisoner's Dilemma in Oligopoly
examples.code:
Two airlines: Fly-Co and Air-X. Both can charge $400 or $250.

If both charge $400: Each earns $1,000,000 profit.
If both charge $250: Each earns $400,000 profit.
If Fly-Co charges $250 while Air-X charges $400:
  Fly-Co captures more passengers: earns $1,200,000.
  Air-X loses passengers: earns $200,000.

Each airline has an incentive to undercut the other.
Result: both end up at $250, earning less than if
they had cooperated at $400.
This is the prisoner's dilemma logic.

questions.q: In perfect competition, why do firms earn zero economic profit in the long run?
options: Governments impose taxes on excess profits, redistributing earnings from firms back to the general public.
Positive economic profits attract new entrants who expand supply and drive the market price down until profits are eliminated.
Firms in perfect competition are legally prohibited from setting prices above their average total cost of production.
Firms voluntarily reduce prices to attract additional customers and build long-term brand loyalty and market share.
questions.explanation: Economic profit signals opportunity. In a competitive market with free entry, profits attract new firms. Increased supply drives down price until the profit opportunity is fully competed away. Long-run equilibrium is zero economic profit, not zero accounting profit.

questions.q: What is a natural monopoly?
options: A monopoly that emerged through voluntary market competition without any government assistance or protection.
A market where one firm can supply all demand at lower average cost than any combination of smaller competing firms, due to large economies of scale.
A market dominated by a single entrepreneur whose talent and innovation have made all competitors unable to survive.
A firm that holds exclusive ownership over a naturally occurring resource that is essential and has no substitutes.
questions.explanation: Natural monopolies arise when economies of scale are so extensive that a single firm always has lower average costs than any combination of smaller firms. Splitting the market between multiple firms raises total average costs, making competition economically inefficient.

questions.q: What is deadweight loss in a monopoly?
options: The profit transferred from consumers to the monopolist through higher prices, representing income redistribution.
The net loss to society from mutually beneficial transactions that do not occur because the monopolist restricts output below the competitive level.
The economic cost of government enforcement and regulatory oversight required to monitor and control monopoly behavior.
The decline in product quality and innovation that results when a single firm faces no competitive pressure to improve.
questions.explanation: Deadweight loss is destroyed value, not transferred value. Under monopoly, output is restricted below the competitive level. Buyers who would have paid more than the cost of production are priced out. Those lost transactions represent a net welfare loss to society that no one captures.

questions.q: What is the key feature that distinguishes oligopoly from other market structures?
options: Firms sell strongly differentiated products, giving each one significant and independent pricing power in its own niche.
There is only one seller in the market, allowing that firm to set prices across the full range of market demand.
Firms are strategically interdependent, meaning each firm must consider and anticipate how rivals will respond to its decisions.
Entry into the market is completely free and costless, ensuring that economic profits are rapidly competed away.
questions.explanation: Strategic interdependence is what defines oligopoly. Firms are large enough that each one's decisions directly affect the others. This interdependence requires game theory to analyze, unlike competitive markets where any single firm's actions have negligible effect on rivals.

questions.q: How does monopolistic competition differ from perfect competition?
options: Monopolistic competition involves fewer buyers in the market, which gives sellers more leverage over pricing decisions.
In monopolistic competition, firms sell differentiated products and therefore have some pricing power, rather than selling identical products at the market price.
In perfect competition, products are strongly differentiated and firms invest heavily in branding and advertising.
Monopolistic competition firms exit the industry in the short run when they earn losses, while competitive firms can remain.
questions.explanation: Product differentiation is the defining difference. In monopolistic competition, each firm's product is slightly distinct, giving it a small downward-sloping demand curve and some pricing power. In perfect competition, products are identical and firms are pure price takers.

questions.q: Why does collusion between oligopolists tend to break down?
options: Government antitrust agencies always discover and dismantle cartel agreements before they can affect market prices.
Each firm has an individual incentive to undercut the agreed-upon price to gain market share, even though if all firms do this, all earn lower profits.
Oligopolists inherently prefer lower prices because they believe this maximizes their long-run customer base and brand loyalty.
Collusion is always detected immediately by consumers who then boycott the colluding firms and purchase substitutes instead.
questions.explanation: The prisoner's dilemma logic applies. Even when firms agree to charge high prices collectively, each has a private incentive to defect and undercut rivals to capture more sales. When all firms follow this incentive, the cartel collapses and all end up worse than if they had cooperated.

lessons.title: Protocol: Identify Market Structure
lessons.prompt:
A market has the following characteristics:
- 4 large firms control 90% of all sales
- Each firm watches competitor pricing closely before setting its own
- Products are similar but not identical
- Barriers to entry are high

Write the market structure this describes: perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, or monopolistic competition.

validation.message: error: a small number of large firms with strategic interdependence, high barriers to entry, and significant market concentration is the definition of oligopoly. the strategic watching of competitor pricing is the key behavioral signal.

lessons.title: Supply and Demand in Labor Markets
lessons.content:
Labor is a factor of production bought and sold in markets. Like goods markets, labor markets are governed by supply and demand, but with some important differences.

The demand for labor is derived demand: it comes from the demand for the goods and services that labor produces. Firms hire workers not because they want workers but because they need workers to produce things buyers want. When demand for a product rises, demand for the labor that makes it also rises.

A firm's demand for labor depends on the marginal revenue product of labor (MRP), which is the additional revenue generated by hiring one more worker. MRP equals the marginal product of labor times the price of the output. Firms hire workers as long as the MRP of each worker exceeds the wage they must pay.

The supply of labor in any particular market comes from workers who choose to offer their services at various wage levels. Higher wages attract more workers into a market. Workers face a tradeoff between work (income) and leisure.

The equilibrium wage is where quantity of labor demanded equals quantity of labor supplied. Factors that shift labor demand include changes in product demand, productivity, and the prices of other inputs. Factors that shift labor supply include changes in population, immigration, education levels, and wages in alternative occupations.

Monopsony is the labor market analog of monopoly: a single buyer of labor rather than a single seller. A company town where one employer dominates hiring has monopsony power. A monopsonist pays workers below their MRP and hires fewer workers than a competitive market would, extracting a surplus at workers' expense. In a monopsonistic market, a minimum wage can actually increase both wages and employment up to the competitive level.

examples.label: Marginal Revenue Product
examples.code:
A factory sells chairs for $50 each.

Worker 1 produces 10 chairs/day.
MRP = 10 x $50 = $500/day.
If wage is $300/day, hiring is profitable.

Worker 5 produces 4 chairs/day (diminishing returns).
MRP = 4 x $50 = $200/day.
If wage is $300/day, not profitable to hire worker 5.

The firm hires up to the worker whose MRP = wage.

lessons.title: Human Capital and Wage Inequality
lessons.content:
Human capital is the stock of knowledge, skills, and education that workers accumulate. Just as physical capital makes workers more productive, human capital increases a worker's productivity and earning potential.

Investment in human capital includes formal education, vocational training, on-the-job experience, and health maintenance. These investments have costs (tuition, time) and returns (higher lifetime earnings). Human capital theory predicts that workers who invest more in education should earn higher wages because they are more productive.

The college wage premium is the difference in earnings between college graduates and high school graduates. This premium has grown substantially over recent decades, reflecting increased demand for skilled workers relative to unskilled workers, driven largely by skill-biased technological change.

Wage inequality is driven by multiple factors: technological change that complements high-skill workers, globalization that exposes domestic low-skill workers to lower-wage foreign competition, declining union membership, and differences in human capital accumulation.

Discrimination is another source of wage inequality. When workers of equal productivity are paid differently based on race, gender, or other characteristics unrelated to productivity, this is labor market discrimination. It reduces individual welfare and creates economic inefficiency by misallocating labor.

Signaling theory offers an alternative interpretation of education's wage premium. Rather than building skills directly, education may primarily signal pre-existing ability to employers who cannot directly observe worker productivity before hiring. If signaling dominates human capital formation, the social return on education spending may be lower than the private return -- an important policy distinction.

examples.label: Return on Education Investment
examples.code:
4-year college degree:
Cost: $120,000 tuition + $140,000 opportunity cost = $260,000

Average wage premium over career: $25,000/year more
than high school graduate.

Over 30 years: $750,000 extra earnings.

Return on investment: ($750,000 - $260,000) / $260,000
= approximately 188% return over a career.

This is why economists treat education as an investment,
not just consumption.

lessons.title: Unions and the Minimum Wage
lessons.content:
Labor unions are organizations of workers that collectively bargain with employers for wages, benefits, and working conditions. By bargaining collectively, workers have more leverage than they would as individuals.

Unions can raise wages for their members above the market equilibrium. This benefits union members but can reduce overall employment in the unionized sector if wages rise above the market-clearing level. The wage differential between union and non-union workers is a subject of substantial economic research.

The minimum wage is a government-set price floor in the labor market. Standard supply and demand analysis predicts that a minimum wage above the equilibrium wage creates unemployment: employers hire fewer workers at the higher mandated wage. However, empirical research on the minimum wage shows more complex results, particularly when employers have monopsony power. Economists David Card and Alan Krueger's study comparing New Jersey and Pennsylvania after a minimum wage increase found no significant employment reduction -- evidence that labor markets are not always perfectly competitive.

The employment effects of minimum wage depend on: how far above the market wage the minimum is set, the elasticity of labor demand, the degree of employer market power, and local market conditions. A minimum wage that is modest relative to local wages may have small employment effects; one set far above equilibrium will create more visible unemployment.

examples.label: Minimum Wage Analysis
examples.code:
Labor market for restaurant workers:
Equilibrium wage: $12/hour
Government minimum wage: $15/hour

At $15/hour:
Quantity of labor demanded: 800,000 workers
Quantity of labor supplied: 1,100,000 workers

Surplus of labor (unemployment): 300,000 workers
want jobs at $15/hour but cannot find them.

Counter-argument: If employers had monopsony power,
they were paying below competitive wages anyway.
A minimum wage could move them closer to
the efficient outcome, not away from it.

questions.q: What is derived demand in the context of labor markets?
options: Demand for labor that arises from government employment programs designed to reduce the unemployment rate.
Demand for labor that originates from consumer demand for the goods and services that labor is used to produce.
Demand for labor that automatically increases whenever wages fall below a threshold level in the market.
Demand for highly skilled labor that consistently exceeds the demand for workers in unskilled occupations.
questions.explanation: Labor demand is not direct. Firms hire workers to produce goods that consumers want to buy. When demand for those goods rises, demand for the workers who make them also rises. The demand is derived from the product market -- labor is an input, not a final good.

questions.q: What is the marginal revenue product of labor?
options: The average revenue generated per worker, calculated by dividing total firm revenue by the number of employees.
The total revenue produced by the entire workforce when all workers are operating at peak productivity.
The additional revenue a firm earns from hiring one more worker, equal to that worker's marginal product times output price.
The minimum wage a firm must pay to attract one additional worker away from their next best employment option.
questions.explanation: MRP is the revenue contribution of the marginal worker: their marginal product (additional output) multiplied by the price of that output. Firms hire workers as long as MRP exceeds the wage. When MRP equals the wage, the firm has found its profit-maximizing employment level.

questions.q: What is human capital?
options: The physical equipment, machinery, and factory space that firms use in the production of goods and services.
The accumulated knowledge, skills, education, and experience that make workers more productive and increase their earning potential.
The total financial wealth held by workers in savings accounts, pension funds, and investment portfolios.
The social networks and professional connections that help workers find better-paying job opportunities over time.
questions.explanation: Human capital is the productive capacity embedded in people through education, training, and experience. Like physical capital, it is an investment with costs and returns. Human capital theory explains why wages differ across workers and why education raises earning potential.

questions.q: Under what condition might a minimum wage increase employment rather than reduce it?
options: When the minimum wage is set exactly at the market equilibrium wage so that no workers are priced out of the market.
When employers have monopsony power and are already paying workers below the competitive wage, so the floor moves wages toward the efficient level.
When the government simultaneously subsidizes employers for every worker they hire at the new minimum wage rate.
When the economy is growing rapidly enough that increased productivity offsets any cost increase from higher mandated wages.
questions.explanation: In a competitive labor market, a minimum wage above equilibrium causes unemployment by standard theory. But if employers have monopsony power, they are already suppressing wages below the competitive level. A minimum wage can push wages toward the competitive equilibrium, potentially increasing both wages and employment.

lessons.title: Externalities
lessons.content:
An externality is a cost or benefit that falls on a party not involved in a transaction. When externalities exist, the private costs or benefits that drive market decisions diverge from the social costs or benefits that reflect total impact on everyone.

A negative externality occurs when a transaction imposes costs on third parties. A factory that pollutes a river imposes costs on downstream residents who had no role in the production decision. The factory's private cost is below the social cost, so it overproduces from society's perspective.

A positive externality occurs when a transaction generates benefits for third parties. Vaccination benefits not just the person vaccinated but everyone around them through reduced transmission. The vaccinated person's private benefit is below the social benefit, so vaccination is underdemanded.

The efficient solution to externalities is to make the decision-maker face the full social cost or benefit. For negative externalities, a Pigouvian tax equal to the external cost per unit internalizes the harm. For positive externalities, a subsidy equal to the external benefit encourages socially optimal production.

The Coase Theorem states that if property rights are clearly defined and transaction costs are low, private parties can negotiate an efficient solution to externality problems without government intervention. If the factory has the right to pollute, downstream residents can pay it to reduce pollution. If residents have the right to clean water, the factory must compensate them or stop polluting. Either assignment of rights reaches efficiency. The theorem breaks down when transaction costs are high (many affected parties, difficulty organizing) -- which is why most real externality problems require policy responses.

examples.label: Negative Externality: Carbon Emissions
examples.code:
A power plant produces electricity.
Private cost: fuel, labor, equipment = $0.06/kWh
External cost: climate damage from emissions = $0.04/kWh
Social cost = $0.10/kWh

Without intervention:
The plant prices at $0.06 and produces too much.
The external cost is borne by society, not the plant.

Pigouvian tax of $0.04/kWh:
Raises the plant's private cost to $0.10/kWh.
Reduces output to the socially optimal level.
Internalizes the externality.

lessons.title: Public Goods and Common Resources
lessons.content:
Goods can be classified along two dimensions: excludability (can people who do not pay be prevented from using it?) and rivalry (does one person's use reduce availability for others?).

A private good is both excludable and rival. A sandwich: if you eat it, I cannot, and I can be excluded from it if I do not pay.

A public good is neither excludable nor rival. National defense protects everyone within a country simultaneously, and one person's protection does not reduce protection for others. Because public goods are non-excludable, the free rider problem arises: rational individuals have no incentive to pay for them voluntarily, since they cannot be excluded from enjoying them regardless.

The free rider problem means public goods tend to be underprovided by private markets. This provides a rationale for government provision financed by taxes.

A common resource is rival but non-excludable. A fishery in international waters: your fishing reduces fish available to me, but neither of us can be excluded from fishing. Without coordination, common resources tend to be overexploited. This is the Tragedy of the Commons.

Elinor Ostrom, who won the Nobel Prize in Economics in 2009, showed that communities often develop effective governance mechanisms to manage common resources without either privatization or government regulation -- through local rules, monitoring, and graduated sanctions. Her work challenged the standard conclusion that commons inevitably collapse.

examples.label: Good Classification
examples.code:
National defense: Non-excludable, non-rival = Public good
(free rider problem, government provides)

A sandwich: Excludable, rival = Private good
(markets work well)

A crowded highway: Non-excludable, rival = Common resource
(congestion = tragedy of commons)

Cable TV (with subscription): Excludable, non-rival = Club good
(natural monopoly tendency, often private)

lessons.title: Information Problems
lessons.content:
Markets work best when both buyers and sellers have full information. When one side of a transaction has significantly more information than the other, market failure can result.

Adverse selection occurs before a transaction, when hidden information causes markets to function poorly. In health insurance, people with health problems are more likely to buy insurance than healthy people. Insurers cannot perfectly observe each customer's health status. The mix of customers is therefore worse than the overall population. This forces insurers to raise premiums, which drives out healthier people, worsening the pool further. In extreme cases, markets can unravel entirely.

George Akerlof's market for lemons shows this with used cars. Sellers know the true quality of their car; buyers do not. Buyers therefore offer only average quality prices. Sellers of high-quality cars refuse to sell at average prices. Only below-average quality cars remain for sale. Buyers anticipate this and lower their offer further. The market fails to sell high-quality used cars.

Moral hazard occurs after a transaction, when having insurance or a guarantee changes behavior. People drive more recklessly when they have comprehensive car insurance. Banks take more risks when they believe the government will bail them out.

Solutions to information problems include signaling (actions taken by the informed party to credibly communicate their type), screening (devices used by the uninformed party to sort among types), and mandatory disclosure requirements. Warranties, professional certifications, and audited financial statements are all market responses to information asymmetry.

examples.label: Signaling in Labor Markets
examples.code:
Employers cannot directly observe a job applicant's productivity.
This is an information asymmetry problem.

A college degree serves as a signal.
Not because education always directly increases productivity,
but because completing college is harder for lower-ability
people than higher-ability people.

The signal separates types: employers use degree
as a screening device. High-ability workers invest
in the signal to distinguish themselves.

This is signaling, not necessarily human capital formation.
The distinction matters for whether education spending
generates social returns beyond private returns.

questions.q: What is a negative externality?
options: A situation where a market produces too little output because producers cannot capture the full value their product creates.
A cost imposed on third parties not involved in the transaction, which the private market does not account for in its pricing.
A government tax that reduces market output below the level that would be produced in an unregulated free market.
A situation where the market price falls below the equilibrium level, creating excess demand and shortages.
questions.explanation: Negative externalities mean social costs exceed private costs. Producers do not account for the harm imposed on uninvolved parties, so they produce more than is socially optimal. Pollution is the classic example -- the factory bears production costs but not the downstream damage it causes.

questions.q: Why are public goods underprovided by private markets?
options: They require extremely costly production processes that private firms cannot afford to undertake profitably.
Consumers place no value on goods that are available to everyone, so willingness to pay is too low to sustain production.
Because they are non-excludable, individuals can free ride and enjoy the good without paying, making private provision unprofitable.
Governments always intervene to provide public goods before private markets can develop, crowding out private producers.
questions.explanation: The free rider problem means rational individuals will not voluntarily pay for public goods they can enjoy regardless of payment. Without revenue, private firms cannot profit. Markets systematically underprovide public goods, which is why governments typically finance them through taxation.

questions.q: What is the Tragedy of the Commons?
options: A failure by governments to maintain public parks and shared spaces, leading to deterioration of community infrastructure.
A situation where individually rational behavior by users of a common resource leads to collective overexploitation and depletion of that resource.
A condition where monopolies gain control over commonly used goods, restricting access and raising prices above competitive levels.
A tendency for public goods to be overproduced by governments because there is no market price to signal the optimal quantity.
questions.explanation: A common resource is rival but non-excludable. Each individual benefits from using as much as possible, because restraint by one person simply allows others to use more. Rational individual behavior leads to collective ruin through overexploitation.

questions.q: What is adverse selection?
options: The tendency of insurance companies to actively select and retain only the healthiest customers while denying coverage to others.
The pattern where poor decisions by inexperienced managers lead to losses that could have been avoided with better training.
