The pre-transaction problem where hidden information causes markets to disproportionately attract the highest-risk participants.
A pricing strategy where firms charge higher prices to customers who have demonstrated less sensitivity to price changes.
questions.explanation: Adverse selection is a pre-transaction information problem. In insurance, those most likely to need coverage are most likely to buy it, while healthy people opt out. The insured pool is riskier than the general population, driving up premiums and potentially causing market unraveling.

questions.q: A government imposes a tax equal to the external cost per unit of pollution on a polluting factory. What is this policy called?
options: A minimum wage, which sets a floor on what workers must be paid by the factory that generates pollution as a byproduct.
A price ceiling, which prevents the factory from charging consumers more than the regulated maximum price for its output.
A Pigouvian tax, which is designed to internalize a negative externality by raising private costs to reflect social costs.
A trade tariff, which imposes a fee on imported goods to protect domestic producers from foreign competition.
questions.explanation: A Pigouvian tax is specifically designed to internalize a negative externality. By raising the firm's private cost to equal the social cost, the tax induces the firm to produce the socially optimal quantity without direct regulation of output quantities or methods.

questions.q: What does the Coase Theorem conclude about externalities?
options: That government taxation is always the most efficient solution to externality problems regardless of the number of parties involved.
That if property rights are clearly defined and transaction costs are low, private bargaining can reach an efficient outcome without government intervention.
That externalities always require regulatory solutions because markets cannot internalize costs that fall on third parties.
That the party causing the externality should always bear the full cost through mandatory liability rules enforced by courts.
questions.explanation: Coase showed that with clear property rights and low transaction costs, affected parties can negotiate to the efficient outcome regardless of who holds the rights. The theorem matters most in its limits: when transaction costs are high or affected parties are numerous, private bargaining fails and policy is needed.

lessons.title: Protocol: Externality Direction
lessons.prompt:
A beekeeper sets up hives next to an apple orchard. The bees pollinate the apple trees, increasing the orchard's apple yield at no cost to the orchard owner.

Is this a positive or negative externality? Who receives it?

Write: positive or negative.

validation.message: error: the orchard receives an uncompensated benefit from the beekeeper's bees. this is a positive externality. the beekeeper and their customers are the transacting parties; the orchard owner is the third party receiving a benefit they did not pay for and were not part of the original transaction.

lessons.title: Gross Domestic Product
lessons.content:
Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given period of time, usually a year or quarter.

The word 'final' matters. GDP counts the sale of a loaf of bread, not the sale of the flour used to make it. Counting the flour would be double-counting: the flour's value is already embedded in the bread's price.

GDP can be measured three ways that produce the same result. The expenditure approach adds up all spending on final goods: consumption (C), investment (I), government spending (G), and net exports (X - M). So GDP = C + I + G + (X - M).

Consumption is household spending on goods and services. Investment is spending by firms on capital goods and inventories (not financial investment). Government spending includes federal, state, and local government purchases but not transfer payments like Social Security, which are redistributions, not purchases of goods. Net exports is exports minus imports.

Nominal GDP is measured in current prices. Real GDP adjusts for inflation to allow comparisons across time. GDP per capita is GDP divided by population -- a rough measure of average living standards.

GDP has significant limitations as a welfare measure. It does not capture income distribution (a country can have high GDP with most of it going to a few people), unpaid work (household labor, childcare), environmental degradation (a country can raise GDP by depleting natural resources), or subjective wellbeing. These limitations have led economists to develop supplementary measures like the Human Development Index and measures of median rather than mean income.

examples.label: GDP Expenditure Components
examples.code:
Hypothetical economy in one year:

Consumption (C): $14 trillion
(households buying food, cars, services)

Investment (I): $3.5 trillion
(firms buying equipment, building factories, inventory)

Government (G): $3.5 trillion
(government buying military equipment, road construction)

Net Exports (X-M): -$0.5 trillion
(imports exceed exports by $0.5 trillion)

GDP = 14 + 3.5 + 3.5 + (-0.5) = $20.5 trillion

lessons.title: Unemployment
lessons.content:
The unemployment rate is the percentage of the labor force that is not employed but is actively seeking work. To be counted as unemployed, a person must be without a job and actively looking for one. People who have given up looking are not counted in the labor force and therefore do not appear in the unemployment rate.

The labor force participation rate measures the fraction of the adult population that is either employed or actively seeking employment. A falling unemployment rate can actually mask labor market weakness if it reflects workers giving up searching rather than finding jobs.

There are three main types of unemployment. Frictional unemployment is the natural result of people moving between jobs. Workers who quit to find a better job, or new graduates searching for their first position, are frictionally unemployed. Some frictional unemployment is healthy because it reflects a dynamic economy.

Structural unemployment occurs when a mismatch develops between worker skills and the skills employers need. Technological change can make entire job categories obsolete, leaving workers with outdated skills unemployed even when jobs exist elsewhere. Structural unemployment requires retraining and adjustment over time.

Cyclical unemployment occurs due to downturns in the overall economy. When GDP contracts, firms cut production and lay off workers. This unemployment declines as the economy recovers.

The natural rate of unemployment is the rate when the economy is at full employment: it includes frictional and structural unemployment but not cyclical. Most economists estimate it around 4-5% for the United States. Full employment does not mean zero unemployment.

examples.label: Types of Unemployment Examples
examples.code:
Frictional:
A nurse quits her hospital job to find one closer to home.
She is job searching for 3 weeks.
This is normal labor market turnover.

Structural:
A coal miner loses his job as the region switches
to renewable energy. His skills do not transfer easily.
He needs retraining for a different sector.

Cyclical:
An autoworker is laid off during a recession
because car sales plummeted.
He would be rehired if the economy recovered.

lessons.title: Inflation and Price Levels
lessons.content:
Inflation is a sustained rise in the overall price level in an economy. When the general price level rises, each dollar buys fewer goods and services. The purchasing power of money falls.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is the most commonly used measure of inflation. It tracks the price of a representative basket of goods and services purchased by a typical household over time.

The inflation rate is the percentage change in the CPI over a period. If the CPI was 260 last year and is 267 this year, the inflation rate is (267-260)/260 = 2.7%.

Moderate inflation (around 2%) is generally considered healthy. It gives central banks room to cut interest rates during recessions, and mild inflation encourages spending over hoarding cash.

Deflation (falling prices) sounds beneficial but is often damaging. When prices fall, consumers delay purchases expecting further price drops. This reduces spending, which reduces production, which lowers prices further. This deflationary spiral can be very difficult to escape, as Japan experienced in the 1990s.

Hyperinflation is extremely rapid inflation that destroys the value of money. Germany in the 1923 and Zimbabwe in the 2000s are historical examples.

Inflation hurts those on fixed incomes and creditors (who are repaid in dollars worth less than when they lent). It benefits debtors (who repay in cheaper dollars) and owners of real assets. The distinction between anticipated and unanticipated inflation is critical: if inflation is fully anticipated, contracts adjust and the harm is limited. Unanticipated inflation redistributes wealth arbitrarily.

examples.label: Who Wins and Loses from Inflation
examples.code:
You lend $10,000 at 3% interest for 10 years.
Inflation averages 5% per year.

Real interest rate = nominal rate - inflation rate
= 3% - 5% = -2%

In real terms, the money you get back is worth less
than what you lent. The borrower wins.
The lender loses.

Unexpected inflation redistributes wealth from creditors
to debtors and from those on fixed incomes
to those with assets whose value rises with prices.

lessons.title: The Business Cycle
lessons.content:
Economies do not grow at a steady pace. They fluctuate between periods of expansion and contraction. These fluctuations are called the business cycle.

A recession is a period of significant decline in economic activity across the economy, lasting more than a few months. Technically, it is often defined as two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth. During recessions, GDP falls, unemployment rises, and business investment contracts.

A depression is a severe, prolonged recession. The Great Depression of the 1930s saw US GDP fall by about 30% and unemployment reach 25%.

An expansion or recovery is a period of rising economic activity. GDP grows, unemployment falls, and consumer and business confidence improve.

The causes of business cycles are debated. They can be triggered by demand shocks (sudden changes in spending), supply shocks (sudden changes in production costs, like an oil price spike), financial crises, or changes in consumer and business confidence.

Automatic stabilizers are features of the tax and spending system that automatically reduce the severity of recessions without requiring deliberate government action. Unemployment insurance automatically pays out more during recessions, supporting consumer spending. Taxes automatically fall as incomes fall, leaving more money in private hands. These stabilizers operate immediately, without the legislative delays that slow discretionary fiscal policy.

Leading economic indicators are variables that tend to change before the economy changes direction: building permits, stock prices, and new manufacturing orders. Lagging indicators change after the economy: unemployment and bank loan rates. Coincident indicators move with the economy: payroll employment and industrial production.

examples.label: Business Cycle Phases
examples.code:
Expansion:
GDP growing, unemployment falling,
business investment rising, consumer confidence high.

Peak:
Economy at its strongest. GDP growth may slow.
Inflation pressure may build as capacity fills.

Recession:
GDP declining, unemployment rising,
investment falling, confidence falling.

Trough:
Economy at its weakest.
Conditions for recovery begin to develop.

Recovery:
GDP begins rising again from the trough.
Slow at first, then accelerating.

questions.q: What does GDP measure?
options: The total income earned by all citizens of a country, including income earned while working abroad in foreign economies.
The total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders during a given time period.
The total value of all goods produced within a country, including intermediate goods used in further production.
The total accumulated wealth held by a country's population in financial assets, real estate, and other holdings.
questions.explanation: GDP counts final goods and services produced within borders during the period. It excludes intermediate goods to avoid double-counting, and it counts production within borders rather than production by citizens wherever they happen to be working.

questions.q: Why is real GDP a better measure of economic growth than nominal GDP?
options: Real GDP grows more rapidly than nominal GDP, making economic progress appear stronger in official statistics.
Real GDP adjusts for price level changes, so it measures actual changes in the quantity of goods and services produced rather than just price increases.
Nominal GDP counts only manufactured goods while real GDP also includes services, making it a more complete measure.
Real GDP incorporates government transfer payments while nominal GDP excludes them, providing a more accurate income measure.
questions.explanation: If prices rise but actual production does not, nominal GDP rises without any real improvement in living standards. Real GDP strips out the price effect using a price index, measuring only genuine changes in the quantity of goods and services produced.

questions.q: A worker has been unemployed for 14 months and has stopped actively searching for work. How is this worker classified?
options: As unemployed and counted in the official unemployment rate, reflecting their continued inability to find suitable work.
As structurally unemployed, because the long duration indicates their skills no longer match available job openings.
As not in the labor force, and therefore not counted in the unemployment rate, because they are not actively seeking work.
As frictionally unemployed, because they are between jobs and their situation reflects normal labor market transitions.
questions.explanation: To be counted as unemployed, a worker must be actively seeking work. Someone who has stopped searching is classified as out of the labor force, not unemployed. This is why the official unemployment rate can understate true labor market weakness during prolonged downturns.

questions.q: What type of unemployment would a travel agent experience after online booking platforms make their job largely obsolete?
options: Frictional unemployment, because the agent is simply between jobs and will find equivalent employment elsewhere soon.
Cyclical unemployment, because the decline in travel agent jobs correlates with broader downturns in economic activity.
Seasonal unemployment, because demand for travel agents fluctuates predictably with travel patterns across calendar months.
Structural unemployment, because a permanent mismatch has emerged between their skills and the jobs now available in the economy.
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment results from a lasting mismatch between worker skills and what employers need, often caused by technological change. A travel agent whose skills are rendered obsolete by technology faces structural unemployment that cannot be resolved just by the economy recovering.

questions.q: Who is hurt most by unexpected inflation?
options: Borrowers who repay loans in dollars that are worth less in real terms than when they originally took out the loan.
Owners of real estate and other physical assets whose nominal values tend to rise with the general price level.
Creditors and people on fixed incomes who receive payments in dollars that are worth less in real terms than anticipated.
Exporters who sell goods abroad and benefit from currency depreciation that often accompanies domestic inflation.
questions.explanation: Unexpected inflation erodes the real value of fixed nominal payments. Creditors who lend at a fixed interest rate receive repayment in dollars worth less than when they lent. People on fixed wages or pensions see their purchasing power fall as prices rise around them.

questions.q: What are automatic stabilizers and why are they valuable during recessions?
options: Government programs that automatically cut spending when deficits reach a certain level, protecting debt sustainability.
Features of the tax and spending system that automatically expand during recessions to support demand without requiring new legislation.
Central bank tools that automatically adjust interest rates in response to changes in the inflation rate.
International agreements that automatically trigger aid transfers when a country's GDP falls below a minimum threshold.
questions.explanation: Automatic stabilizers like unemployment insurance and income taxes work without legislative action. During recessions, unemployment payments rise and tax collections fall, both supporting private spending. Their speed advantage over discretionary policy is that they operate immediately as economic conditions change.

lessons.title: Protocol: Calculate GDP
lessons.prompt:
An economy has the following data for one year:

Consumer spending: $800 billion
Business investment: $150 billion
Government purchases: $200 billion
Exports: $100 billion
Imports: $120 billion

Using the expenditure approach, calculate GDP.

Write only the number in billions (example format: $1,000 billion).

validation.message: error: GDP = C + I + G + (X - M) = 800 + 150 + 200 + (100 - 120) = 800 + 150 + 200 + (-20) = $1,130 billion. imports are subtracted because they represent spending on foreign production, not domestic output.

lessons.title: Government Spending and Taxation
lessons.content:
Fiscal policy refers to the use of government spending and taxation to influence the overall economy. It is the primary macroeconomic tool available to elected governments.

Expansionary fiscal policy is used during recessions to stimulate economic activity. It involves increasing government spending, decreasing taxes, or both. More government spending directly adds to aggregate demand. Lower taxes leave more money in household and business hands, which tends to increase consumption and investment.

Contractionary fiscal policy is used when the economy is overheating, producing high inflation. It involves decreasing government spending, increasing taxes, or both. This removes money from the economy, reducing spending and slowing price increases.

The multiplier effect is the phenomenon where an initial change in spending leads to a larger total change in GDP. If the government spends $100 billion, those who receive that money spend some of it, those who receive that spending spend some of it, and so on. The total increase in GDP exceeds the initial $100 billion. The size of the multiplier depends on the marginal propensity to consume (MPC): how much of each additional dollar of income people spend rather than save. Multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC).

Crowding out occurs when government borrowing to finance spending drives up interest rates, reducing private investment. If the government borrows heavily, it competes with private borrowers for available funds, pushing interest rates up. Higher interest rates make private investment projects less attractive.

Fiscal policy operates with lags. A recognition lag: time to identify a problem. A legislative lag: time to pass a spending or tax change. An implementation lag: time for the policy to take effect. By the time fiscal stimulus is implemented, the recession may already be ending, meaning the stimulus arrives procyclically rather than countercyclically.

examples.label: The Multiplier Effect
examples.code:
Marginal propensity to consume (MPC) = 0.8
Government increases spending by $100 billion.

Round 1: Government spends $100 billion.
Recipients spend 80% = $80 billion.

Round 2: Those recipients spend 80% = $64 billion.

Round 3: $51.2 billion... and so on.

Multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / (1 - 0.8) = 5

Total increase in GDP = $100 billion x 5 = $500 billion.
Initial $100 billion generates $500 billion in total output.

lessons.title: Budget Deficits and National Debt
lessons.content:
A budget deficit occurs when a government spends more than it collects in tax revenue in a given year. A budget surplus occurs when revenue exceeds spending.

When a government runs a deficit, it must borrow to finance the gap. It does this by selling government bonds, which are IOUs promising to repay the borrowed amount plus interest at a future date.

The national debt is the cumulative total of all past deficits minus all past surpluses. The United States national debt exceeds $30 trillion.

Whether deficits are harmful depends on context. A small deficit during a recession is generally considered acceptable. Persistent large deficits during economic expansions are more concerning.

Debt sustainability is the key concern. A government can maintain high debt if its economy grows faster than the interest rate it pays on debt. The debt-to-GDP ratio is the standard measure. High debt levels constrain future fiscal policy because a larger share of government revenue must go to interest payments.

Ricardian equivalence is the controversial proposition that deficit financing and tax financing of government spending have identical effects on the economy. The logic: rational households, knowing that deficits imply future taxes, save the full amount of any tax cut to pay those future taxes. If true, fiscal multipliers are zero. Most economists believe Ricardian equivalence holds only partially in practice because households are credit-constrained, have finite horizons, and do not perfectly anticipate future tax changes.

examples.label: Deficit vs Debt
examples.code:
Year 1: Government spends $5 trillion, collects $4 trillion in taxes.
Deficit = $1 trillion. Borrows $1 trillion.
Debt = $1 trillion.

Year 2: Government spends $5.2 trillion, collects $4 trillion.
Deficit = $1.2 trillion. Borrows $1.2 trillion.
Debt = $1 trillion + $1.2 trillion = $2.2 trillion.

Year 3: Surplus of $0.5 trillion (collects $5.7, spends $5.2).
Pays down $0.5 trillion of debt.
Debt = $2.2 trillion - $0.5 trillion = $1.7 trillion.

The deficit is the annual gap. The debt is the running total.

lessons.title: Tax Systems
lessons.content:
Taxes are the primary source of government revenue and a key tool of fiscal policy. How a tax is designed affects both its revenue generation and its economic effects.

A progressive tax takes a higher percentage of income from higher earners. The US federal income tax is progressive: the first bracket of income is taxed at 10%, higher brackets at higher rates. Progressive taxes reduce income inequality.

A regressive tax takes a higher percentage of income from lower earners than from higher earners. Sales taxes are regressive: everyone pays the same rate on purchases, but since lower-income households spend a larger share of their income, the tax consumes a larger fraction of their income.

A proportional (flat) tax takes the same percentage of income from everyone regardless of income level.

The Laffer Curve describes the relationship between tax rates and tax revenue. At a tax rate of 0%, revenue is zero. At a rate of 100%, revenue is also zero (no one would work if all income were confiscated). In between, there is some rate that maximizes revenue. The key empirical debate is not whether the curve exists but where the current rate is relative to its maximum.

Tax incidence refers to who actually bears the burden of a tax, which may differ from who officially pays it. The actual distribution of burden depends on the elasticities of supply and demand. If demand is inelastic, buyers cannot easily avoid the good when prices rise, so they bear more of the burden. If supply is inelastic, sellers cannot reduce production easily, so they bear more of the burden. The statutory incidence (who writes the check) is economically irrelevant.

examples.label: Tax Incidence
examples.code:
Government taxes sellers $2 per gallon of gasoline.
Before tax: price = $3, quantity = 100 gallons.

Sellers pass on $1.50 of the tax by raising price to $4.50.
Sellers absorb $0.50, reducing their net price from $3 to $2.50.

Buyer burden: +$1.50 (price rise)
Seller burden: -$0.50 (lower net price)

The tax burden is split because demand for gas is somewhat
inelastic: buyers cannot easily avoid it.
More inelastic demand = buyers bear more of the tax burden.

questions.q: What is expansionary fiscal policy?
options: Increasing tax rates to generate additional government revenue that can be used to reduce the national debt faster.
Increasing government spending or decreasing taxes to add to aggregate demand and stimulate economic activity during a downturn.
Raising the central bank's target interest rate to slow inflation by reducing the money available for borrowing.
Decreasing the money supply to slow economic overheating and reduce upward pressure on consumer prices.
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy adds to aggregate demand. By spending more or taxing less, the government puts more money into the economy, supporting output and employment during downturns. It operates through the government spending and taxation levers, not through monetary policy tools.

questions.q: What is the multiplier effect?
options: The tendency for government tax revenues to increase faster than spending during expansions, automatically reducing deficits.
The phenomenon where an initial change in spending generates a larger total change in GDP as money circulates through the economy.
The increase in national debt caused by compound interest accumulating on outstanding government bond obligations.
The increase in import spending that results from rising domestic incomes during an economic expansion.
questions.explanation: Money spent cycles through the economy multiple times. Each recipient spends some fraction (the MPC), creating income for others, who spend some fraction, and so on. The initial injection generates a multiple of itself in total GDP change, with the multiplier equal to 1/(1-MPC).

questions.q: What is the difference between the national debt and the annual budget deficit?
options: They measure the same fiscal condition but are reported using different accounting methodologies by different agencies.
The deficit is the annual shortfall between spending and revenue; the national debt is the cumulative total of all past deficits minus surpluses.
The national debt is fully repaid each fiscal year while the annual deficit carries forward into the following budget cycle.
The deficit is owed exclusively to foreign governments while the national debt encompasses all creditors including domestic ones.
questions.explanation: The deficit is a flow: how much more than revenues the government spends in one year. The debt is a stock: the total accumulated borrowing over all years, minus any repayment. Running a surplus reduces debt; running a deficit adds to it.

questions.q: What does it mean for a tax to be regressive?
options: The statutory tax rate decreases automatically as an individual's taxable income rises through successive income brackets.
Lower-income households pay a higher fraction of their income in tax than higher-income households, even if the nominal rate is the same.
The tax was introduced in a previous legislative session and has since become outdated relative to current economic conditions.
The tax is broadly unpopular with voters and politically difficult to defend, making it a liability for the party that enacted it.
questions.explanation: A regressive tax burden falls more heavily, as a share of income, on lower earners. Even if the rate is technically the same for everyone, if lower-income people spend a larger fraction of their income on taxed goods, the tax consumes a larger percentage of their total income.

questions.q: What is crowding out?
options: The process by which new firms entering a profitable market reduce the profits available to existing incumbent firms.
The effect where government borrowing drives up interest rates, making private investment projects more costly and reducing private capital formation.
The reduction in purchasing power that savers experience when inflation rises faster than the interest earned on their savings.
The displacement of domestic production that occurs when cheaper imported goods replace domestically manufactured products.
questions.explanation: When the government borrows heavily to finance spending, it competes with private borrowers for available funds. This competition drives up interest rates, which makes private investment more costly and causes some profitable projects to be cancelled or deferred.

questions.q: A tax on gasoline is imposed on sellers. Gasoline demand is very inelastic. Who bears most of the tax burden?
options: Sellers bear most of the burden because the tax is officially and legally imposed on them as the producers and distributors.
Buyers bear most of the burden because inelastic demand means they cannot meaningfully reduce consumption when prices rise.
The government bears most of the burden because collecting an inelastic-demand tax generates less revenue than expected.
Foreign oil producers bear most of the burden because domestic producers pass all cost increases back through the supply chain.
questions.explanation: Tax incidence depends on elasticity, not on who legally pays the tax. When demand is inelastic, buyers continue purchasing despite price rises. Sellers can pass most of the tax onto buyers through higher prices without losing much volume. The more inelastic the demand, the more buyers bear the burden.

lessons.title: Protocol: Fiscal Policy Direction
lessons.prompt:
The economy is in a deep recession. GDP has fallen for three consecutive quarters. Unemployment is at 9%.

Should the government use expansionary or contractionary fiscal policy to address this situation?

Write one word: expansionary or contractionary.

validation.message: error: a recession calls for expansionary fiscal policy: increase government spending, cut taxes, or both. this adds to aggregate demand, supporting output and employment. contractionary policy removes demand from the economy and would deepen the recession.

lessons.title: Money and Banking
lessons.content:
Money is anything widely accepted as a medium of exchange. It solves the problem of barter, which requires a double coincidence of wants: both parties must have exactly what the other wants at the same time.

Money performs three functions. It is a medium of exchange (used to buy and sell things), a store of value (can be saved and used later), and a unit of account (a common measure for pricing goods and comparing values).

M1 is the narrowest measure of money supply: physical currency in circulation plus demand deposits (checking accounts). M2 adds savings accounts, money market accounts, and small time deposits.

Commercial banks create money through the money multiplier process. Banks are required to keep a fraction of their deposits as reserves and can lend out the rest. When a bank loans out deposits, those funds are deposited in other banks, which loan out most of that, and so on.

If the reserve requirement is 10%, an initial deposit of $1,000 can support up to $10,000 in total deposits through the banking system. The money multiplier is 1 / reserve requirement.

The Federal Reserve (the Fed) is the central bank of the United States. It regulates the banking system, serves as a lender of last resort to prevent bank failures from cascading, and conducts monetary policy to manage the macroeconomy.

Banking system fragility is a key feature: banks borrow short (deposits can be withdrawn immediately) and lend long (mortgages and business loans span years). This maturity mismatch creates vulnerability to bank runs. If depositors collectively try to withdraw simultaneously, even a solvent bank can fail. Deposit insurance (FDIC in the US) was introduced to prevent runs by assuring depositors they will be repaid regardless.

examples.label: Money Multiplier
examples.code:
Reserve requirement = 10% (banks must keep 10% of deposits).
Initial deposit: $1,000.

Bank A keeps $100, lends $900.
Bank B receives $900, keeps $90, lends $810.
Bank C receives $810, keeps $81, lends $729.
...

Total deposits created = $1,000 x (1/0.10) = $10,000.

Money multiplier = 1 / reserve requirement = 10.
A single $1,000 deposit supports $10,000 in total money supply.

lessons.title: How the Fed Controls the Money Supply
lessons.content:
The Federal Reserve uses three primary tools to conduct monetary policy, though in practice one dominates.

Open market operations are the Fed's main tool. The Fed buys or sells government bonds on the open market. When it buys bonds, it pays by crediting bank reserves, expanding the money supply and pushing interest rates down. When it sells bonds, it removes reserves from the banking system, contracting the money supply and pushing rates up.

The federal funds rate is the interest rate banks charge each other for overnight loans of reserves. The Fed targets this rate through its open market operations. Changes in the federal funds rate ripple through to all other interest rates in the economy: mortgage rates, car loan rates, business loan rates, and savings rates.

The discount rate is the interest rate the Fed charges commercial banks that borrow directly from it. It serves as a signal and a backstop, but banks rarely use it as a primary funding source.

Reserve requirements set the minimum fraction of deposits banks must hold. Lowering requirements allows banks to lend more from the same deposits, expanding money supply. This tool is rarely used.

Quantitative easing (QE) is an unconventional tool used when the federal funds rate is near zero and conventional cuts are exhausted. The Fed buys long-term assets (mortgage-backed securities, long-term Treasury bonds) to inject reserves and push down long-term interest rates. QE was used extensively after the 2008 financial crisis and again during the COVID-19 pandemic.

examples.label: Open Market Operations
examples.code:
Economy in recession. Fed wants to stimulate:

Fed buys $100 billion in government bonds.
Pays by crediting bank reserve accounts.
Banks have more reserves than required.
They lend out the excess.
Money supply expands through the multiplier.
Interest rates fall.
Cheaper borrowing stimulates investment and spending.

Fed wants to fight inflation:
Fed sells $100 billion in bonds.
Banks pay by draining their reserve accounts.
Fewer reserves means less lending capacity.
Money supply contracts. Interest rates rise.
Higher borrowing costs reduce spending and investment.

lessons.title: Inflation Targeting and Central Bank Independence
lessons.content:
Most modern central banks operate with a target inflation rate. The Federal Reserve's target is 2% annual inflation. This target provides a clear anchor for expectations: businesses and workers can plan ahead knowing the Fed will act to keep inflation near 2%.

When inflation rises above target, the Fed raises interest rates to cool spending and reduce price pressure. When inflation falls below target or the economy contracts, the Fed lowers interest rates to stimulate activity.

The dual mandate is the Fed's legal obligation to pursue both price stability (low inflation) and maximum employment. These goals sometimes conflict. Fighting inflation by raising rates can increase unemployment. Supporting full employment can risk generating inflation.

Central bank independence means the central bank makes monetary policy decisions without direct control by elected politicians. Independence is considered important because politicians face electoral incentives to stimulate the economy before elections, which can generate inflation. An independent central bank can take unpopular steps like raising rates without fear of electoral consequences.

The Phillips Curve describes a short-run tradeoff between inflation and unemployment: lower unemployment tends to be associated with higher inflation as a tight labor market pushes up wages and therefore prices. In the long run, many economists believe this tradeoff disappears and unemployment returns to its natural rate regardless of the inflation rate -- the long-run Phillips Curve is vertical. This means monetary policy can affect real output in the short run but only inflation in the long run.

examples.label: Fed Response to High Inflation
examples.code:
2022 scenario:
Inflation rises to 8%, well above the 2% target.
Unemployment is at 3.5%, near historic lows.

Fed response: aggressive rate hikes.
Fed funds rate raised from 0.25% to over 5% in 18 months.

Effect:
Borrowing costs rise sharply.
Mortgage rates rise from 3% to over 7%.
Business investment slows.
Consumer spending on credit falls.
Inflation eventually falls toward 3-4%.
Cost: unemployment rises modestly.

questions.q: What are the three functions of money?
options: Store of value, unit of account, and medium of exchange.
Asset accumulation, liability settlement, and equity creation.
Saving for the future, spending on consumption, and investing in productive capital.
Physical currency, electronic credit creation, and capital market financing.
questions.explanation: Money serves three functions: it facilitates exchange (medium of exchange), can be stored for future use (store of value), and provides a common measure for prices and comparisons (unit of account). All three distinguish money from barter and from non-monetary stores of value like art.

questions.q: When the Federal Reserve buys government bonds in open market operations, what happens to the money supply?
options: The money supply decreases because the Fed is absorbing bonds from the market, reducing available financial assets.
The money supply remains unchanged because the Fed simply swaps one type of asset for another of equal value.
The money supply increases as bank reserves are credited and banks gain additional capacity to extend loans.
Interest rates rise because the increased demand for bonds pushes bond prices down and yields upward.
questions.explanation: When the Fed buys bonds, it pays by crediting banks' reserve accounts. More reserves support more lending and money creation through the money multiplier. The money supply expands, and excess reserves put downward pressure on the federal funds rate.

questions.q: What is the primary monetary policy tool used by the Federal Reserve?
options: The reserve requirement, which determines the fraction of deposits banks must hold and cannot lend out.
The discount rate, which is the interest rate the Fed charges banks that borrow directly from the Federal Reserve.
The federal funds rate, targeted through open market operations that buy or sell government bonds.
The exchange rate, adjusted through currency market interventions to influence import and export competitiveness.
questions.explanation: The federal funds rate is the Fed's principal lever. By influencing this rate through buying and selling government bonds, the Fed affects all other interest rates in the economy, thereby influencing borrowing, spending, and investment across the entire economy.

questions.q: Why do most economists support central bank independence?
options: Because central banks produce more accurate economic forecasts than government agencies due to better data access.
Because independent central banks can take necessary but politically unpopular actions, like raising interest rates, without electoral consequences.
Because independent central banks operate with lower administrative costs than politically directed monetary authorities.
Because governments fundamentally lack the technical expertise to understand how monetary policy transmission mechanisms work.
questions.explanation: Politicians face electoral incentives to stimulate economies before elections regardless of whether stimulus is appropriate. An independent central bank can raise rates when necessary to fight inflation without worrying about electoral outcomes, providing credibility that anchors inflation expectations.

questions.q: What is the short-run tradeoff described by the Phillips Curve?
options: Lower inflation is associated with lower interest rates, making monetary policy an effective tool for simultaneously achieving both goals.
Lower unemployment tends to be associated with higher inflation as tight labor markets generate wage and price pressure.
Higher GDP growth consistently leads to higher unemployment as firms substitute capital for labor to meet rising production demands.
Lower interest rates are reliably associated with lower inflation by reducing the cost of holding money and encouraging spending.
questions.explanation: The Phillips Curve shows that in the short run, policies that reduce unemployment tend to generate more inflation, and vice versa. Policymakers face a tradeoff. The long-run Phillips Curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment, meaning the tradeoff disappears over time.

questions.q: What is quantitative easing?
options: The practice of printing additional physical currency and distributing it to households to stimulate consumer spending.
A reduction in the reserve requirement percentage that allows commercial banks to lend a larger fraction of their deposits.
Large-scale central bank purchases of longer-term assets to inject reserves and push down long-term rates when short-term rates are near zero.
An increase in the federal funds rate target used to tighten monetary conditions and reduce inflationary pressure.
questions.explanation: Quantitative easing is used when conventional interest rate cuts are exhausted because rates are near zero. The central bank buys long-term assets to inject reserves and push down long-term rates, providing stimulus through non-traditional channels that conventional rate policy cannot reach.

lessons.title: Protocol: Monetary Policy Direction
lessons.prompt:
Inflation is running at 7%, well above the 2% target. The economy is at full employment.

Should the Federal Reserve use expansionary or contractionary monetary policy?

Write one word: expansionary or contractionary.

validation.message: error: high inflation well above target calls for contractionary monetary policy. the Fed raises interest rates to reduce borrowing, slow spending, and reduce price pressure. expansionary policy would add to demand and worsen the inflation problem further.

lessons.title: Why Countries Trade
lessons.content:
International trade allows countries to consume beyond their own production possibilities by specializing in goods where they are relatively more efficient and trading for the rest.

Absolute advantage is the ability to produce a good using fewer resources than another country. But absolute advantage is not what determines trade patterns.

Comparative advantage is the ability to produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country. Even if one country has an absolute advantage in producing everything, both countries gain from specializing in goods where their opportunity cost is lowest and trading.

The source of comparative advantage comes from differences in factor endowments (the Heckscher-Ohlin model), technology differences, economies of scale, and historical accidents of industrial development.

The gains from trade are real and substantial. The increase in total world output from specialization is divided between trading partners through the terms of trade: the relative prices at which goods exchange internationally. Both countries gain as long as the terms of trade lie between each country's domestic opportunity cost ratios.

Free trade is not without distributional effects. While it increases total output and consumer welfare (through lower prices and greater variety), it also displaces workers in import-competing industries. The gains are spread widely across many consumers; the losses are concentrated on specific workers and communities. This asymmetry explains why free trade generates political opposition even when its aggregate effects are positive.

examples.label: Comparative Advantage Example
examples.code:
Country A can produce per worker per day:
  10 units of wheat OR 5 cars.
  Opportunity cost of 1 car = 2 units of wheat.

Country B can produce per worker per day:
  6 units of wheat OR 4 cars.
  Opportunity cost of 1 car = 1.5 units of wheat.

Country B has comparative advantage in cars (lower opp. cost).
Country A has comparative advantage in wheat.

Both countries gain by specializing and trading
even though Country A is absolutely better at wheat.

lessons.title: Trade Barriers and Policy
lessons.content:
Despite the gains from free trade, governments frequently impose trade barriers. Understanding why and what they cost is central to international economics.

A tariff is a tax on imported goods. It raises the domestic price of the imported good, reducing imports and benefiting domestic producers at the expense of domestic consumers. The government collects revenue. The net effect on welfare is negative when the country is small and cannot affect world prices.

A quota is a limit on the quantity of a good that can be imported. It has similar price and quantity effects to a tariff but generates no government revenue -- the gains go instead to whoever holds the import licenses.

Subsidies to domestic producers lower their costs, allowing them to compete with cheaper imports without an explicit trade barrier. They create a cost to taxpayers rather than consumers.

Arguments for trade protection include the infant industry argument (new industries need temporary protection to develop scale and learn), national security (some industries must be maintained regardless of cost), and strategic trade policy (governments can use subsidies or protection to give domestic firms advantages in oligopolistic industries with learning curves and economies of scale).

The World Trade Organization (WTO) provides a framework for multilateral trade negotiations and dispute resolution. Regional trade agreements (like NAFTA/USMCA, the EU single market) reduce barriers among member countries while potentially diverting trade from more efficient non-member producers.

examples.label: Tariff Effects
examples.code:
World price of steel: $400/ton.
Domestic demand: 1,000 tons. Domestic supply: 400 tons.
Imports = 600 tons at $400.

Government imposes $100/ton tariff:
Domestic price rises to $500/ton.
Domestic demand falls to 900 tons.
Domestic supply rises to 600 tons.
Imports fall to 300 tons.

Winners: domestic steel producers (higher price, more sales).
Government: $100 x 300 tons = $30,000 in revenue.
Losers: domestic consumers (pay more, buy less).
Net welfare loss: the deadweight loss triangles.

lessons.title: Exchange Rates and the Balance of Payments
lessons.content:
An exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another. Exchange rates are determined in foreign exchange markets by supply and demand for currencies, though governments sometimes intervene.

When a currency appreciates (rises in value relative to others), imports become cheaper for domestic consumers, but exports become more expensive for foreign buyers. This tends to reduce exports and increase imports, worsening the trade balance. Depreciation has the opposite effect.

The balance of payments is an accounting record of all economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world. It has two main components. The current account records trade in goods, trade in services, income from foreign investments, and transfer payments. The capital (financial) account records investment flows: foreign direct investment, portfolio investment, and changes in reserve assets.

A fundamental identity: the current account deficit must be financed by a capital account surplus, and vice versa. A country that imports more than it exports must be borrowing from abroad (capital inflow) to finance the gap. The US runs a persistent current account deficit, financed by foreigners investing in US assets.

Purchasing power parity (PPP) is the theory that exchange rates should adjust so that identical goods cost the same in different countries. In practice, PPP is a long-run tendency, not a short-run fact. The Economist's Big Mac Index uses the price of McDonald's Big Macs internationally to illustrate deviations from purchasing power parity.

examples.label: Exchange Rate Effects on Trade
examples.code:
US firm exports products priced at $100 each.
Exchange rate: 1 USD = 1 euro.
European buyers pay 100 euros per unit.

USD appreciates: 1 USD = 1.20 euros.
European buyers now pay 120 euros for the same product.
US exports become more expensive. Demand from Europe falls.

USD depreciates: 1 USD = 0.85 euros.
European buyers now pay 85 euros for the same product.
US exports become cheaper. Demand from Europe rises.

Depreciation helps exporters. Appreciation hurts them.

questions.q: What is comparative advantage?
options: The ability to produce a good using absolutely fewer resources than any other country can, regardless of what else is being produced.
The ability to produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country, determining which goods each country should specialize in.
The advantage a country gains by having a larger workforce and more capital equipment than its main trading partners.
The benefit a country receives from having established trade relationships and negotiated preferential tariff rates with partners.
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage is about opportunity cost, not absolute efficiency. Even if one country is better at everything, both gain from each specializing where their opportunity cost is lowest. Absolute advantage is irrelevant to the gains from trade.

questions.q: What is the effect of a tariff on domestic consumers?
options: Consumers benefit from lower prices as the tariff revenue is returned to citizens through government spending programs.
Consumers are unaffected because domestic producers absorb the full cost of the tariff rather than passing it to buyers.
Consumers are harmed by higher prices and reduced quantity of the imported good available in the domestic market.
Consumers gain greater product variety as tariffs encourage foreign firms to establish domestic production facilities.
questions.explanation: A tariff raises the domestic price of the imported good. Domestic consumers pay more and consume less. Some consumer surplus is transferred to domestic producers and some to government as tariff revenue, but a deadweight loss represents a net welfare reduction.

questions.q: If a country runs a current account deficit, what must be true of its capital account?
options: The capital account must also be in deficit because both accounts respond to the same underlying economic conditions.
The capital account must show a surplus, because the current account deficit must be financed by net inflows of foreign capital.
The capital account balance is completely unrelated to the current account and determined by separate financial factors.
The capital account must be in balance because central bank reserves automatically adjust to eliminate any discrepancy.
questions.explanation: The balance of payments must balance. A current account deficit means the country is importing more than it exports, so it must be borrowing from abroad -- a capital inflow -- to finance the difference. Current account deficits and capital account surpluses are two sides of the same transaction.

lessons.title: Protocol: Identify Comparative Advantage
lessons.prompt:
Country X can produce 20 tons of grain OR 10 tons of timber per worker per year.
Country Y can produce 15 tons of grain OR 9 tons of timber per worker per year.

Which country has the comparative advantage in timber?

Write: Country X or Country Y.

validation.message: error: calculate opportunity cost of timber for each country. Country X: 1 ton of timber costs 20/10 = 2 tons of grain foregone. Country Y: 1 ton of timber costs 15/9 = 1.67 tons of grain foregone. Country Y gives up less grain per ton of timber, so it has the comparative advantage in timber.

lessons.title: The Economics of Climate Change
lessons.content:
Climate change is the largest externality problem in economic history. Burning fossil fuels imposes costs on current and future generations worldwide, but those costs are not reflected in the price of fuel. The result is that fossil fuels are overconsumed from society's perspective.

Carbon pricing is the primary market-based solution proposed by economists. A carbon tax puts a price on carbon emissions equal to the estimated social cost of carbon. This raises the private cost of burning fossil fuels to equal the social cost, reducing consumption to the optimal level and generating revenue the government can use or return to citizens.

A cap and trade system sets a total cap on carbon emissions and issues permits equal to that cap. Firms can trade permits. Firms that can reduce emissions cheaply sell their permits to firms that cannot. The total level of emissions is controlled, and reductions happen where they are cheapest, minimizing total cost.

The social cost of carbon is an estimate of the economic damages caused by emitting one additional ton of CO2. Estimates range from $50 to $200+ per ton depending on assumptions about discount rates, climate sensitivity, and economic impacts.

Discount rates are central to climate economics. Future damages must be discounted to present value for cost-benefit analysis. High discount rates make future damages seem small, reducing the urgency of action. Low discount rates make future damages loom large. This is as much a philosophical choice about obligations to future generations as an economic one. The Stern Review (2006) used low discount rates and found aggressive action justified; critics argued the discount rate assumptions were too low.

examples.label: Carbon Tax vs Cap and Trade
examples.code:
Both aim to reduce carbon emissions.

Carbon tax:
- Sets price of emissions (e.g., $50/ton CO2)
- Quantity of emissions is uncertain (depends on response)
- Revenue goes to government
- Predictable cost for businesses

Cap and trade:
- Sets quantity of emissions (total cap)
- Price of permits is uncertain (fluctuates)
- Revenue from permit auctions goes to government
- Guarantees meeting the emissions target

Both are more economically efficient than
command-and-control regulations that dictate
specific technologies or behaviors.

lessons.title: Automation and the Future of Work
lessons.content:
Technological change has always disrupted labor markets. The Industrial Revolution automated agricultural and artisan work. The computer revolution automated clerical and manufacturing work. Today, artificial intelligence and robotics are automating a widening range of cognitive tasks.

Economists generally believe that technological change, over the long run, creates more jobs than it destroys. New technologies increase productivity, which increases real incomes, which increases demand for goods and services, which creates demand for workers. The Luddites who smashed weaving machines in 19th century England were eventually proven wrong: employment in textiles grew after mechanization.

However, the short and medium run can involve significant disruption. Workers whose specific skills become obsolete face structural unemployment that requires retraining. The gains from automation tend to be concentrated among capital owners and high-skill workers who complement technology, while the losses are concentrated among routine-task workers.

The debate about AI is particularly intense because AI potentially automates cognitive tasks across many sectors simultaneously, affecting a broader range of workers than previous waves. Whether this time is fundamentally different or follows historical patterns is genuinely uncertain.

Skill-biased technological change is the phenomenon where technology increases demand for high-skill workers relative to low-skill workers, contributing to rising wage inequality. This has been a dominant force in labor markets since roughly 1980 in advanced economies.

Policy responses include investment in education and retraining, strengthening portable benefits not tied to specific employers, and potentially broader redistribution through the tax system to share productivity gains from automation more widely.

examples.label: Historical Pattern of Automation
examples.code:
1900: 41% of US workforce in agriculture.
2020: 1.4% of US workforce in agriculture.

Huge productivity gains from mechanization.
Most farm jobs eliminated.
Yet total employment rose dramatically.

Where did the workers go?
Manufacturing, services, healthcare, education,
technology, entertainment, finance.
These sectors grew because agricultural
productivity gains raised incomes
and created demand for new products.

Historical lesson: technology destroys specific jobs
but creates broad economic growth that generates
demand for different types of work.

lessons.title: Housing Markets
lessons.content:
Housing is one of the most important markets in any economy, accounting for roughly a third of consumer spending when rent and related costs are included. It is also one of the most regulated, and the consequences of those regulations are widely debated.

Housing supply is highly inelastic in many cities due to land scarcity, zoning regulations, and long construction timelines. When demand for housing rises, prices rise substantially if supply cannot keep pace.

Zoning laws determine what can be built where. Restrictions on multifamily housing, minimum lot sizes, height limits, and parking requirements all limit housing supply. Economists broadly agree that restrictive zoning in high-demand cities is a major cause of housing unaffordability and contributes to inequality by pricing workers out of high-productivity cities.

Rent control is a price ceiling applied to housing. It creates a shortage, reduces housing quality over time, and can freeze tenants in place because moving means losing below-market rent. It benefits current tenants at the expense of future renters and the overall housing stock.

The economic consequences of housing unaffordability extend beyond individual households. When workers cannot afford to live near high-productivity cities, labor cannot flow to its most productive uses. Research by economists Chang-Tai Hsieh and Enrico Moretti estimated that land use restrictions in high-productivity cities (New York, San Jose, San Francisco) reduced US GDP by 2-9% relative to a counterfactual with fewer restrictions -- a massive aggregate economic cost from local zoning decisions.

examples.label: Housing Supply and Price
examples.code:
City A (low regulation): New housing built freely.
Demand increases as tech sector grows.
Supply also increases: new apartments constructed.
Price rise is modest. Workers can afford to move in.

City B (high regulation): Strict zoning, limited construction.
Demand increases as tech sector grows.
Supply barely increases: few permits granted.
Prices spike dramatically.
Low and middle-income workers cannot afford to stay.
City segregates by income.

Same demand shock, opposite outcomes due to supply response.

lessons.title: Healthcare as an Economic Problem
lessons.content:
Healthcare markets fail in ways that distinguish them from ordinary goods markets. Understanding these failures explains why healthcare is so heavily regulated and why pure market provision is considered inadequate by most economists.

Information asymmetry is severe. Patients do not know what treatments they need. They must rely on providers who both recommend and supply treatment, creating a principal-agent problem where the agent (doctor) has different incentives than the principal (patient).

Insurance creates moral hazard: insured patients consume more healthcare than they would if paying full price. Insurance markets also suffer from adverse selection, where the sick disproportionately seek coverage, raising premiums and driving out healthy participants.

Healthcare has significant positive externalities (vaccination prevents transmission to others) and is often considered a right rather than just a good, creating political pressure for universal access.

The US spends more than any other high-income country on healthcare as a share of GDP yet achieves average or below-average health outcomes on many measures. This is partly explained by high administrative costs, provider market concentration giving hospitals and physician groups pricing power, and fee-for-service payment models that reward volume of treatment rather than health outcomes.

Universal healthcare systems (Canada, UK, most of Europe) address market failures through government provision or strict regulation. They achieve lower per-capita spending while covering entire populations. The tradeoffs involve potential wait times, limits on some treatments, and different innovation incentives.

examples.label: Market Failures in Healthcare
examples.code:
1. Information asymmetry:
Patient does not know if surgery is necessary.
Doctor recommends surgery and also performs it.
Conflict of interest embedded in the market.

2. Adverse selection:
Sick people want insurance. Healthy people opt out.
Insurance pool is skewed. Premiums rise.
More healthy people exit. Premiums rise further.

3. Moral hazard:
With insurance, a patient chooses the MRI
rather than monitoring. It costs them the same.
But it raises costs for all insureds.

4. Externalities:
Unvaccinated person can infect others.
Private decision, public health consequence.

lessons.title: Inequality and Redistribution
lessons.content:
Income inequality has risen substantially in most advanced economies since the 1980s. Understanding its causes and consequences is one of the central debates in contemporary economics.

The Gini coefficient is the standard measure of income inequality. It ranges from 0 (perfect equality, everyone has the same income) to 1 (perfect inequality, one person has all income). The United States has a Gini coefficient around 0.4, among the highest of advanced economies.

Causes of rising inequality include: skill-biased technological change favoring high-skill workers, globalization increasing competition for routine jobs, declining unionization reducing worker bargaining power, winner-take-all dynamics in superstar markets, and policy changes including tax code shifts.

The relationship between inequality and economic growth is contested. Some economists argue high inequality reduces growth by limiting human capital investment among lower-income households and reducing consumer spending. Others argue inequality reflects returns to skill and risk-taking that are essential to innovation. The empirical evidence is mixed and context-dependent.

Redistribution mechanisms include progressive taxation, transfer payments (social insurance programs, means-tested benefits), public provision of education and healthcare, and minimum wage regulation. All involve tradeoffs: redistribution can reduce inequality but may also affect work incentives and economic efficiency.

Intergenerational mobility -- the degree to which children's economic outcomes differ from their parents' -- is a complementary concern to snapshot inequality. A society with high inequality but high mobility is different from one with high inequality and low mobility. The United States has less intergenerational mobility than many European countries despite its self-image as a land of opportunity.

examples.label: Gini Coefficient Comparison
examples.code:
Gini coefficients (approximate, market income before redistribution):

Denmark:     0.29 (low inequality)
Germany:     0.31
Canada:      0.34
United Kingdom: 0.36
United States:  0.41
Mexico:      0.46
Brazil:      0.53 (high inequality)

After taxes and transfers, most European countries
reduce inequality substantially.
The US does less redistribution relative to peers,
so post-tax inequality remains high.

Note: pre-tax Gini and post-tax Gini can differ a lot.

questions.q: Why do economists generally prefer carbon pricing over command-and-control environmental regulation?
options: Carbon pricing generates substantially more government revenue than regulatory approaches that impose compliance costs on businesses.
Carbon pricing allows emissions reductions to occur where they are cheapest, minimizing the total economic cost of reaching any given reduction target.
Command-and-control regulations have been proven in every documented case to fail at actually reducing total emissions.
Carbon pricing eliminates all emissions immediately by making fossil fuel combustion prohibitively expensive across all sectors.
questions.explanation: A price on carbon lets markets find the cheapest ways to reduce emissions. Firms that can cut emissions cheaply do so; those for whom cutting is costly pay the price instead. Total emissions fall at minimum cost. Command-and-control rules cannot achieve this efficiency because they cannot know which firms can reduce at lowest cost.

questions.q: Why does restrictive zoning increase housing unaffordability?
options: Zoning raises construction costs by mandating higher quality building materials and more expensive engineering standards.
Restrictive zoning prevents new housing supply from expanding in high-demand areas, so when demand rises, prices absorb the entire adjustment.
Zoning regulations reduce property tax assessments, indirectly making housing more expensive by shifting the tax burden to renters.
Restrictive zoning increases average housing quality by preventing low-cost construction, which raises market prices throughout the area.
questions.explanation: Housing prices are determined by supply and demand. When zoning prevents new construction, supply cannot respond to rising demand. All of the adjustment falls on price rather than quantity, often dramatically pricing out low and middle-income residents from high-productivity areas.

questions.q: What is the principal-agent problem in healthcare?
options: The conflict that arises when insurance companies deny claims that patients and their doctors believe are medically necessary.
The situation where patients lack the financial resources to pay for the care that medical professionals recommend.
The misalignment that occurs when the agent (doctor) who recommends treatment also provides and profits from that treatment, creating potentially conflicting incentives.
The regulatory problem where governments set drug prices below market rates, reducing pharmaceutical companies' incentive to innovate.
questions.explanation: In healthcare, doctors are agents acting on behalf of patients who lack the knowledge to evaluate recommendations. When providers profit from the treatments they recommend, their incentives may not perfectly align with patient welfare. This is a form of information asymmetry combined with a conflict of interest.

questions.q: What does economic history suggest about the long-run employment effects of automation?
options: Automation creates a permanent reduction in total employment as machines permanently replace human labor across all affected sectors.
Automation always and immediately increases total employment because new technologies create new industries faster than they destroy old ones.

Historically, automation has destroyed specific job categories but raised productivity and real incomes, generating demand for new types of work with net positive employment effects over the long run.
The employment effects of automation are essentially random and cannot be predicted by studying historical patterns of technological change.
questions.explanation: Historical waves of automation (agricultural mechanization, factory machines, computers) destroyed specific job categories but did not cause permanent mass unemployment. Productivity gains raised incomes and demand, creating new employment in sectors that did not previously exist. Whether AI follows this pattern is a genuine current debate.

questions.q: What is a cap and trade system for carbon emissions?
options: A regulatory system where the government sets a maximum allowable price for carbon-intensive energy to protect consumers from price spikes.
A government program that provides subsidies and tax credits to firms that invest in renewable energy and clean technology.
A system that sets a binding total cap on emissions and allows firms to buy and sell permits, so reductions occur wherever they are cheapest.
A regulatory framework that bans the most carbon-intensive industries and mandates their replacement with approved cleaner alternatives.
questions.explanation: Cap and trade controls total emissions by limiting the number of permits. Trading allows efficiency: firms that can cut emissions cheaply sell permits to those for whom cutting is costly. The environmental goal (the cap) is met at the lowest possible economic cost.

questions.q: What does the Gini coefficient measure and what does a higher value indicate?
options: It measures average income growth over time, with higher values indicating faster economic expansion and rising living standards.
It measures income inequality on a scale from 0 to 1, with higher values indicating greater inequality in the distribution of income.
It measures the share of GDP generated by the top income quintile, with higher values indicating a more productive high-skill sector.
It measures intergenerational income mobility, with higher values indicating that children's incomes are less correlated with their parents' incomes.
questions.explanation: The Gini coefficient ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (all income goes to one person). Higher values mean greater inequality. It is the standard summary measure for income distribution comparisons across countries and over time, though it captures only one dimension of a complex distribution.

lessons.title: Protocol: Market Failure Identification
lessons.prompt:
A pharmaceutical company develops a new vaccine that prevents a highly contagious disease. Each person vaccinated not only protects themselves but also reduces the risk of infection for everyone around them.

What type of market failure does this represent, and in which direction will the market underprovide or overprovide the vaccine?

Write: positive externality, underprovide OR negative externality, overprovide.

validation.message: error: vaccination creates positive externalities because benefits flow to third parties not involved in the vaccination decision. because buyers do not capture all the benefits (some go to unvaccinated neighbors), private willingness to pay is below the social benefit. the market will underprovide vaccination relative to the socially optimal level, which is why governments often subsidize or mandate vaccines.
